title: sigstore-keyless signing (W10.1)
body:
Certificates get identities — sigstore-style keyless signing bound to who ran the check (a CI workflow identity or a maintainer's OIDC identity), so a certificate says not just "this was checked" but "this was checked by this identity." Keyless means no long-lived signing keys to leak; the identity comes from an OIDC provider at signing time, which is how modern supply-chain signing sidesteps the key-management disaster entirely.

**Design notes.** sigstore-style OIDC keyless (CI workflow / maintainer identities); `fsl login` local vs CI-OIDC flows; no long-lived keys; the identity layer over M2 signing (W8.19).

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m2-signing`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.1.json`.

title: registry entry format finalized (W10.10)
body:
The registry entry format — what a published machine's registry record contains, where the publish bundle itself IS the entry — finalized now, even though registry hosting waits for v16. Nailing the entry format early means the irreversible part (how a machine is described and identified in a registry) is settled before anyone builds on it, so the ecosystem era can host without having to redesign the record and break everything already published.

**Design notes.** registry ENTRY format final (publish bundle = entry); hosting stays v16; the irreversible record shape settled before the marketplace exists.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `ecosystem-plumbing`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.10.json`.

title: transparency log + equivocation mitigation (W10.2)
body:
An append-only public log that makes certificate claims tamper-evident — plus mitigation for the equivocation attack, where a log shows different views to different people. Offline inclusion proofs alone can't detect such a fork, so the mitigation is witness cosigning or gossip. This is what makes "logged when" actually trustworthy: a claim in the log can't be quietly retracted or shown selectively to one party and hidden from another.

**Design notes.** Rekor-or-self-hosted log (decision from the security brainstorm), inclusion proofs self-contained for offline; equivocation mitigation via witness cosigning or gossip; the tamper-evidence layer.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m2-signing`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.2.json`.

title: revocation + advisory feed format (W10.3)
body:
A revocation mechanism and an advisory feed — so when a checker or runtime turns out to have a vulnerability, the feed identifies the whole blast radius of certificates that need re-certification. Verification you can't revoke is verification you can't trust after a bug is found; the advisory feed (shaped like RustSec or GHSA) is how a checker CVE propagates to "these proofs are now suspect and must be re-run."

**Design notes.** revocation + advisory feed (RustSec/GHSA-shaped), keyed by checker/runtime version; identifies the re-certification blast radius of a checker CVE.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m2-signing`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.3.json`.

title: verify verb full chain (M5) (W10.4)
body:
One verb — `verify` — walks the whole trust sentence: signed by whom, logged when, which rules were certified, run recomputed, revocations checked. Cheap mode trusts the issuer's replay in milliseconds; full mode re-derives everything from scratch. This is milestone 5: the single command that answers "should I believe this certificate," at whatever level of paranoia the situation calls for, from a fast CI gate to a full independent recomputation.

**Design notes.** chain per receipts §7 (sig->identity->log inclusion->cert covers hash->replay(full)->tier evidence->identity binding->revocation->window); `--cheap|--full|--offline` (offline: staleness caveat, never hard-fail); lockfile/dir sweeps default cheap; exit codes per class; jssm twin #829.

Source: MP§25.2. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m5-verify`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.4.json`.

title: receipts replayed slice (M6) (W10.5)
body:
Receipts — attestations about a single run: "this run of machine@hash satisfied property φ, at this authenticity tier, for this subject." The `replayed` tier ships first, and v13's recorded-effect ledger is what makes even hooked machines attestable. Receipts are diplomas for computations: compliance gates, agent-to-agent trust, provable claims about what a specific run actually did — with tiers that can't be faked because the evidence is what produces them.

**Design notes.** record per receipts §5 (signed core + optional bundle); issuance = replay + φ-gate + sign (false claims unmintable); `replayed` tier first (v13 ledger, W9.2); jssm twin #830.

Source: MP§25.2. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m6-receipts`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.5.json`.

title: hosted + witnessed tiers + provenance tape ext (W10.6)
body:
The higher receipt authenticity tiers — `hosted` (the receipt records the execution context) and `witnessed` (per-stimulus provenance signatures, an additive tape extension). Tiers can't be inflated because each is produced by stronger evidence: a witnessed receipt exists only if every input was provenance-signed as it arrived. This is the ladder from "I replayed it myself" up to "an independent witness attested every input as it happened."

**Design notes.** `hosted` (execution_context) + `witnessed` (per-stimulus provenance sigs, additive tape extension) tiers; tiers produced by evidence, not asserted; extends the receipt ladder above `replayed` (W10.5).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W10.5.

Source: MP§25.2. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m6-receipts`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.6.json`.

title: holder-key layer C + presentation challenge (W10.7)
body:
Identity layer C — holder-key presentations with a fresh challenge, so the party presenting a receipt proves they hold the key it was issued to, defeating replay of a stolen receipt. This is what lets a receipt function as a credential you present, like a verifiable credential: the fresh-challenge presentation proves possession, so a leaked receipt can't be reused by anyone who merely copied the bytes.

**Design notes.** identity layer C (holder-key) + fresh-challenge presentations; defeats receipt-replay; the credential-presentation layer (layers B external / A in-tape sit below it).

Source: MP§25.2. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m6-receipts`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.7.json`.

title: trusted event time (W10.8)
body:
Trusted event-time — available only at the witnessed tier — so a receipt can make a defensible claim about when something happened, not merely that it happened. Time is only as trustworthy as its source; binding trusted time to the witnessed tier (where inputs are already provenance-signed) is what keeps "this ran before the deadline" from being a claim anyone could forge by lying about their own clock.

**Design notes.** trusted event-time only at the witnessed tier (W10.6); defensible when-claims; bound to provenance-signed inputs.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W10.6.

Source: MP§25.2. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `m6-receipts`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.8.json`.

title: fsl.lock schema + sweeps (W10.9)
body:
`fsl.lock` — reproducible system builds where every registry member is pinned to its source hash, certificate, and edition, sweepable in CI so a build can't silently drift. This is the supply-chain keystone, specced before the first incident instead of after: the lockfile is what makes "the exact same verified components" a checkable fact, and `verify` sweeps enforce that every locked certificate stays valid over time.

**Design notes.** schema (irreversibles #18): members[{name, source_hash, certificate_ref, edition, resolved_path}]; lock-verify in `verify` sweeps; receipts participate ("these receipts must stay valid"); refinement certs flow through upgrades.

Source: MP§25.2. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `ecosystem-plumbing`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W10.9.json`.

title: host ABI finalized (W11.1)
body:
The boundary every runtime implements — the host ABI — gets frozen, now including v13's effect and persistence surface. This is the contract for exactly how a host provides hooks, sensors, channels, time, and randomness to a machine, how errors and cancellation and rollback are represented, and how replay injects recordings. Freezing it is what makes "write once, run on any host" real: a runtime that implements this ABI level runs any machine at that level.

**Design notes.** ABI = hook/sensor/channel call shapes, time + random sources, error representation, cancellation, rollback notification, replay injection, recording format; versioned; adapters declare ABI level; incorporates the era-4 durability surface (W9.14).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W9.14.

Source: MP§16. Part of #1410 (v15 umbrella). cluster `abi-migration`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W11.1.json`.

title: per-host runtimes via the bringup kit (W11.2)
body:
Adding host N stops being a research project — port a roughly 200-line harness, run the conformance corpus, declare your tier. Python and C follow the v6 Rust runtime; demand picks the rest. The bringup kit is what turns "support another language" from a months-long port into a bounded, checklist-driven task, because each runtime is just an IR consumer plus a C2 stepper measured against the shared vectors.

**Design notes.** bringup kit = corpus runner, trace comparator, canonical-value serialization tests, IR loader tests, ABI mocks, negative diagnostics, replay tests, manifest validator; each runtime = IR consumer + C2 stepper (T1 first); tier growth by passing more vectors, never by assertion.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W11.1.

Source: MP§16, MP§26. Part of #1410 (v15 umbrella). cluster `per-host-runtimes`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W11.2.json`.

title: typegen fleet-wide (W11.5)
body:
Every host gets the machine's type surface in its own idiom — C headers, Rust enums, Python type hints — via target plugins, with no certification gate. This scales the v10 TypeScript typegen to the whole fleet: whatever language you consume a machine from, you get compile-time-checked state and action names and data shapes in that language's native types, so the machine is as pleasant and safe to use from C as it is from TypeScript.

**Design notes.** typegen C headers / Rust enums / Python hints via target plugins (no cert gate); the fleet-wide scaling of v10 typegen (W6.106).

Source: MP§16. Part of #1410 (v15 umbrella). cluster `typegen-manifests-wasm`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W11.5.json`.

title: package-name attributes + manifests (W11.6)
body:
Package-name attributes with per-manager overrides, emitted as proper manifests for every package ecosystem (npm, cargo, pip, nuget, composer, gem). This is the fourth build artifact: a machine ships with a real, correct package manifest for whatever registry it is published to, so distributing an FSL machine as a native package becomes a generated step rather than hand-written boilerplate re-derived per ecosystem and inevitably wrong somewhere.

**Design notes.** package_name + per-manager overrides (npm/cargo/pip/nuget/composer/gem…) -> emitted manifests (the 4th artifact); native packaging per ecosystem.

Source: MP§16. Part of #1410 (v15 umbrella). cluster `typegen-manifests-wasm`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W11.6.json`.

title: WASM toolchain productized (W11.8)
body:
The toolchain itself ships as WebAssembly — the Rust core plus the verb surface behind stdio and JS bindings — so FSL runs where agents actually run, with no install. This powers the playground and the editor, and more importantly it means an AI agent or a browser can run the full FSL toolchain (parse, check, run) without a native install, which is decisive for the AI-distribution era where the consumer is a model, not a developer with a dev environment.

**Design notes.** WASM = the Rust core + verb surface behind stdio/JS bindings; powers playground + editor; no-install toolchain; falls out of the v6 Rust implementation (WP10.3).

**Dependencies.** Depends on WP10.3.

Source: MP§16. Part of #1410 (v15 umbrella). cluster `typegen-manifests-wasm`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W11.8.json`.

title: pinned-unicode cross-host (W11.9)
body:
The pinned-Unicode guarantee from v7 verified across the whole fleet — every host agrees on normalization, case, and number formatting, checked by the differential CI. This is the T3 tier's substance: string and number handling is the classic place independent implementations silently disagree, so proving cross-host agreement on the pinned Unicode tables is what lets a machine's text behavior be trusted everywhere it runs, not just on the reference host.

**Design notes.** pinned-unicode + number pinning verified fleet-wide (#815); the T3 tier substance; the differential CI proves cross-host string/number agreement; builds on the v7 pinned normalization (W6.58).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W11.4.

Source: MP§8, MP§26. Part of #1410 (v15 umbrella). cluster `fleet-certification`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W11.9.json`.

title: registry loop-first vertical (W12.1)
body:
Before building any marketplace, prove the research-grade core on a tiny vertical: an agent states a contract, queries, assembles a composite, gets a green proof or a minimal counterexample, and swaps a part under refinement. If that loop isn't cheap enough for an agent's inner loop, the whole thesis is hollow — so prove it on a narrow slice before indexing anything. This is the registry's make-or-break experiment, deliberately done small first rather than built out on faith.

**Design notes.** slice per registry-close: TS/JS only, hierarchical operator only, checkable band only, author-claimed + one registry-verified(ts-js) path, counterexample traces, adapter-as-proof-carrying-component, skeletal third-party adjunct binding (namespace+hash); THEN the wider marketplace.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `registry`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.1.json`.

title: distribution program (W12.10)
body:
Make FSL visible where humans and models look — MCP registries, skills, llms.txt, machines.txt, GitHub Linguist, articles, Q&A seeds, apps — the migrated jssm#831–#869 block, run opportunistically forever and never gating engineering. This is ambient work: the llms.txt and cheat-sheet generate from the same tables as the docs, a Playwright announcer posts releases, and SEO follows the taxonomy. Distribution is a standing background program, not a milestone with an end.

**Design notes.** the migrated jssm#831-#869 block (era-tagged twins); llms.txt/cheat-sheet generated from the docs tables; the Playwright announcer (fsl#1252) posts releases; SEO per the taxonomy doc; version-agnostic, runs any time.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `distribution`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.10.json`.

title: behavioral/typeclass search (W12.2)
body:
Search the registry by behavior, not just by name — find a machine by the interface it satisfies or the properties it proves, a typeclass-style query over what a machine does rather than what it happens to be called. This is what makes a registry of verified machines actually useful: you ask for "something that behaves like a rate limiter with these guarantees" and get proof-carrying matches, not a keyword lottery you then have to audit by hand.

**Design notes.** behavioral/typeclass search over the registry; query by satisfied interface / proven property; trust tiers target-qualified (never bare); competing adjuncts; policy-constrained resolution (SAT/CSP); behavioral semver via proof-dependency invalidation.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W12.1.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `registry`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.2.json`.

title: guardrails-as-product (mcp --policy) (W12.3)
body:
The keynote demo becomes a product — an agent's tool surface gated by a certified machine, so "never charge twice without confirmation" is a proof, not a prompt you hope the model obeys. This is FSL securing the very interface that exposes FSL to AI: tool calls become events, legality becomes a current-state transition check, and every policy decision lands on the tape for audit. A certified policy machine is a guardrail that can't be talked out of its rules by clever phrasing.

**Design notes.** `mcp --policy guard.fsl`; tool calls = events, legality = current-state transition check; policy machines certified + lockfile-pinned; decisions land on the tape (auditable); template library (no-network-after-secret, approval-before-delete, tests-before-deploy).

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `guardrails`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.3.json`.

title: query fleet governance (W12.4)
body:
`query` — ask one behavioral question across every machine in an org, with every hit shipping its witness tape. This is fleet governance: "which of our machines can reach a state that charges a customer without a confirmation step" answered across the whole fleet at once, each answer carrying its proof. It is the audit tool for an organization running hundreds of verified machines that no human could review one at a time.

**Design notes.** `query` predicates (reachable/flow/through/population/edition) over the checker machinery fleet-wide; witness tape per hit; the org-wide governance verb.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `query-patches`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.4.json`.

title: semantic patches (W12.5)
body:
A structured-edit protocol that lets many agents work on one machine without trampling each other's text — semantic patches with source-hash preconditions, dry-run, and behavioral-diff receipts. This is how AI agents collaborate on machines: edits are operations with preconditions and provable behavioral effects, not blind text merges, and the same machinery powers a git semantic-merge driver so even version control understands an FSL change behaviorally rather than line by line.

**Design notes.** semantic patches (§29 door): fsl.patch.v1-style ops, source-hash preconditions, dry-run + behavioral-diff receipts; the git semantic merge driver rides the same machinery.

Source: MP§29. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `query-patches`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.5.json`.

title: infer (aspirational) (W12.6)
body:
Learn a draft machine from production logs — passive automata learning (L*-flavored) over tapes and logs, cleaned up with `minimize`. This is the reverse of everything else: instead of writing a machine and checking its behavior, you observe behavior and derive a candidate machine. Labeled aspirational until the eval bar clears, because inferring a correct machine from examples is genuinely hard, but it completes the ladder — infer learns from behavior, check judges against properties, synth derives from properties.

**Design notes.** infer = passive L*-flavored automata learning over tapes/logs -> draft + `minimize` cleanup (W8.18); stays labeled aspirational until the eval bar clears.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `infer-synth`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.6.json`.

title: synth (aspirational) (W12.7)
body:
Derive a guardrail machine from properties, correct by construction — safety-game solving over Dwyer-pattern specs yields a maximally-permissive policy machine plus a certificate. This is the other aspirational bookend: instead of writing a machine and proving it satisfies a property, you state the property and synthesize a machine that provably does. Aspirational until the eval bar clears, but it is the endpoint the whole verification stack has been pointing toward from the start.

**Design notes.** synth = safety-game solving over Dwyer-pattern specs (decidable safety fragment) -> maximally-permissive policy + certificate; the correct-by-construction bookend; aspirational until eval clears.

Source: MP§25.1. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `infer-synth`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.7.json`.

title: curriculum + eval corpus at scale (W12.8)
body:
The manual, curriculum, and eval program at full size — the book with its v11-unlocked spine, learn-by-repairing as both on-ramp and eval set, the taxonomy's 1,336 seeds filling in, every snippet CI-proven. This is FSL's teaching surface and its own benchmark at once: a corpus of (broken, tape, fixed) tuples that teaches humans and evaluates models, with every example proven correct so the teaching material can never quietly rot into wrongness.

**Design notes.** `test --doc` over markdown fences + doc-comment examples; curriculum = (broken, tape, fixed) tuples with splits + contamination policy; eval suite (NL->FSL, FSL->NL, fix-given-counterexample) versioned; every instance = NL<->FSL corpus pair, canonically formatted.

Source: MP§16. Part of #1411 (v16 umbrella). cluster `corpus`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W12.8.json`.

title: int = s53 semantics + literals (W6.1)
body:
FSL's default integer is a signed 53-bit int — the largest integer JavaScript can represent exactly — so `int` means the identical thing whether the machine runs in a browser, in Node, or in a compiled host, with no silent precision loss at the boundary. Literals come in every base a systems programmer expects (hex, binary, octal) with type suffixes (7f, 7L, 7i32, 7u8) so you write the exact type you mean inline instead of casting after the fact. This is the floor the whole numeric tower stands on.

**Design notes.** s53 = signed 53-bit, JS-exact integer range; literal grammar covers hex/bin/oct + suffixes (f=float, L=longint, iNN/uNN=sized); the default type every other numeric kind promotes to or from explicitly.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.1.json`.

title: bytearray + [< >] literal (W6.10)
body:
A first-class array of bytes with its own literal syntax (`[< ... >]`), for machines that touch binary — protocol frames, hashes, encoded blobs — without smuggling bytes through strings and praying the encoding survives the round trip. Bytes are bytes, kept distinct from text, with a deliberate byte view that the string ops can cross into when you actually mean to.

**Design notes.** bytearray type + `[< >]` literal; distinct from string; byte-view interop with string ops (W6.57); pairs with the encoding builtins (W6.54) and the pinned hash (W6.55).

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `byte-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.10.json`.

title: seeded-walk reproducibility surface (W6.100)
body:
Make a random walk of a machine exactly reproducible from a seed, and carry that seed in the serialization — so "it did X on run 4711" becomes a claim anyone can replay bit-for-bit instead of a story. This is the reproducibility floor the whole replay-and-proof stack stands on: determinism tests promote straight into conformance vectors, and a bug report can ship the seed that triggers it rather than a paragraph of hand-waving.

**Design notes.** seed captured in the serialized form; deterministic PRNG (splitmix lineage) so a walk is a pure function of (machine, seed, stimuli); the M3 determinism tests promote into the C4 vector corpus (WP-1.4, the standing no-normative-feature-without-a-vector rule).

Source: MP§15, era1/WP-1.4. Part of #1401 (v6 umbrella). cluster `portability-contract`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.100.json`.

title: lint verb (W6.101)
body:
The `lint` verb — static analysis of an FSL machine for the problems the grammar accepts but the author probably didn't mean: unreachable states, island subgraphs, undeclared references, style issues. This is the first line of the CLI tooling story and the thing CI runs on every machine; it catches the mistakes that are legal FSL but wrong intent, before they reach a diagram or a user.

**Design notes.** `lint` verb (§25); reachability/island/undeclared-ref/style checks; jssm twin #620; feeds `lint --future` (W6.94).

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.101.json`.

title: parse --json (AST/locations) (W6.103)
body:
`parse --json` emits the parsed AST with source locations as JSON, so any tool in any language can consume FSL structure without embedding the parser. This is the integration primitive: editors, linters, doc generators, and codegen all get a stable, machine-readable view of a machine's syntax tree, with locations precise enough to underline the exact offending token rather than the whole line.

**Design notes.** `parse --json` emits AST + source locations; the interop surface for external tooling; locations feed diagnostics and editor features.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.103.json`.

title: explain + error-code registry (W6.104)
body:
The `explain` verb plus the error-code registry it reads — every FSL error has a stable code, and `explain FSL1234` gives you the full description, cause, and fix. This turns a cryptic error into a teachable one, and because the registry was reserved back in v6 the codes are public API, so tools and docs link directly to an error's explanation instead of fragile pattern-matching on message strings.

**Design notes.** `explain` verb over the error-code registry (reserved in WP6.8); stable `FSL####` codes; projection B6; the human-facing half of the error model.

**Dependencies.** Depends on WP6.8.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.104.json`.

title: list verb (W6.105)
body:
The `list` verb — enumerate the parts of a machine (states, edges, actions, groups, vals) as structured output, so you can ask "what is in this machine" programmatically. Small but load-bearing for tooling and for a human exploring an unfamiliar machine: it is the table of contents you reach for before reading the whole thing.

**Design notes.** `list` verb (§25); enumerate states/edges/actions/groups/vals; structured output; a tooling and exploration primitive.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.105.json`.

title: typegen TS (W6.106)
body:
`typegen` — generate TypeScript type definitions from a machine, so a TypeScript host gets compile-time-checked state names, action names, and data shapes instead of stringly-typed guesses. This is the feature that makes FSL pleasant to consume from TS: your editor autocompletes the machine's states and the compiler catches a typo'd transition name before it runs. TypeScript-first now; the fleet-wide typegen across many languages is era 6.

**Design notes.** `typegen` TypeScript emitter (§16); states/actions/data-shape types; ungated (no verification dependency); the fleet-wide typegen is era 6.

Source: MP§16, MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.106.json`.

title: inspect (bundle reader) (W6.107)
body:
`inspect` — read a compiled FSL bundle and report what is inside it: metadata, version, machines, artifacts. This is the tool for understanding a distributed or serialized machine you didn't author, the compiled-form counterpart to `list` — what you reach for when someone hands you a bundle and asks "what even is this."

**Design notes.** `inspect` verb reads compiled bundles; reports metadata/version/contents; the compiled-form counterpart to `list` (W6.105).

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.107.json`.

title: grammar export gbnf/lark/ebnf (W6.108)
body:
Export FSL's grammar in the formats other tools speak — GBNF (for LLM-constrained generation), Lark, EBNF — so external parsers, generators, and LLM samplers can work with FSL syntax natively. GBNF in particular means a language model can be constrained to emit only valid FSL, which is directly load-bearing for the AI-distribution era. The exports ride the grammar dual, so they stay in sync with the real grammar instead of rotting.

**Design notes.** grammar export to gbnf/lark/ebnf; rides the C3 grammar dual (WP10.1); GBNF enables constrained LLM generation (era-7 distribution); generated, not hand-maintained.

**Dependencies.** Depends on WP10.1.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.108.json`.

title: enum(...) — named closed sets (W6.11)
body:
A named closed set of symbolic values — `enum(red, green, blue)` — for the countless machine variables that are "one of a short list," modeled as a real type instead of magic strings. The checker knows the full set, so an exhaustive `case` over an enum can be proven complete, and an impossible value becomes a compile error rather than a runtime typo that surfaces in production as a state nobody handled.

**Design notes.** closed symbolic set; exhaustiveness-checkable in `case` (W6.41); numeric-backed enums behave per the val numeric-enum fix (Tjssm-759); intensional membership.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `scalar-values`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.11.json`.

title: doc-comment syntax (/// or doc "") (W6.111)
body:
Doc-comment syntax — `///` or `doc "..."` — so a machine's states, transitions, and vals can carry documentation right in the source; grammar-level in this tier, with extraction coming in the docs era. This is the hook that later lets FSL generate reference docs from a machine the way TypeDoc does from code. Here it is just the syntax, reserved now so authors can start writing docs and a later release doesn't have to break to add it.

**Design notes.** doc-comment syntax (`///` or `doc ""`) — grammar only in this tier; extraction/rendering is the era-2 docs-extraction artifact (W7.37); reserving the syntax now avoids a later breaking add.

Source: MP§16. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `cli-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.111.json`.

title: null/undefined JS-distinct, no arithmetic (W6.12)
body:
FSL keeps `null` and `undefined` as distinct values — matching the JavaScript host rather than pretending the world is simpler than it is — and forbids arithmetic on either, so `null + 1` never quietly becomes 1. The two genuinely mean different things (explicitly-absent versus never-set), and conflating them or doing math on them is exactly where host-language bugs leak into machine logic and stay hidden until a guard reads wrong.

**Design notes.** null and undefined distinct, both first-class; arithmetic on either is a compile error; interacts with the .data undefined-setter fix (Tfsl-1264) and T? nullability (W6.25).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.25.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `scalar-values`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.12.json`.

title: tuple (W6.13)
body:
Fixed-length, heterogeneous tuples — `(int, string, bool)` — for the small structured values that don't deserve a named record: a coordinate pair, a key-and-weight, a labeled return. Positional and typed per slot, so the checker knows exactly what sits at each index and a mismatched shape is a compile error rather than a runtime surprise when someone reads position 2 expecting a string.

**Design notes.** fixed-arity heterogeneous tuple; per-position typing; structural; pattern-matchable in case (W6.41).

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `containers`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.13.json`.

title: array + length bounds (W6.14)
body:
Homogeneous arrays with optional length bounds — "a list of at most eight readings" — so the length becomes a type the checker can size, keeping the machine in the finite band. Bounded arrays are exactly what let a machine hold collections and still make finiteness claims; unbounded arrays are available too, for when you genuinely need them and are willing to leave the checkable band to get them.

**Design notes.** homogeneous array; optional length bound -> finite band; bracket/get + HOFs via the container protocol (W6.43).

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `containers`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.14.json`.

title: set #[...] (W6.15)
body:
A set literal `#[a b c]` for unordered, de-duplicated membership — the right type whenever "which things are present" matters and order and count don't. Backed by the container protocol so `in`, map, and filter all work, and value-based so two sets with the same members are equal, which means set equality does the obvious thing instead of comparing insertion order.

**Design notes.** set type + `#[ ]` literal; value equality; membership via `in` (W6.35); container protocol (W6.43).

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `containers`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.15.json`.

title: bag #(...) (petri store, monotone ops) (W6.16)
body:
A multiset — a bag `#(a a b)` that counts multiplicity — with monotone operations, which is exactly the store a Petri-net-style machine needs: tokens accumulate and are consumed, and the monotone discipline keeps the reasoning tractable. This is the data structure behind the whole resource-counting class of machines, where "how many of each" is the state that matters.

**Design notes.** bag/multiset + `#( )` literal; multiplicity counts; monotone ops (petri store); value equality; container protocol (W6.43).

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `containers`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.16.json`.

title: map #{} keys num/str (W6.17)
body:
A map/dictionary `#{k: v}` with numeric or string keys, for the associative lookups every non-trivial machine eventually needs — a routing table, a per-key counter, a name-to-config binding. Keyed access returns an option, so a miss is a value you handle rather than a crash, and the whole structure is value-equal and serializable so it replays with the rest of the state.

**Design notes.** map type + `#{ }` literal; num/str keys; get->option (W6.22); value equality; container protocol (W6.43).

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `containers`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.17.json`.

title: record {} (W6.18)
body:
Named-field records `{ x: 1, y: 2 }` for structured data that deserves field names instead of tuple positions — the workhorse aggregate type. Structurally typed, so a function that wants `{ x, y }` accepts anything carrying those fields, and pattern-matchable so a `case` can destructure a record inline instead of reading fields one accessor at a time.

**Design notes.** record type + `{ }` literal; named fields, structural typing; pattern-matchable (W6.41); value equality.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `containers`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.18.json`.

title: object-removal guard (compile error + message) (W6.19)
body:
A guard that turns "you removed a field or state something still depends on" into a compile error with a message naming what broke, instead of a runtime `undefined` surfacing three steps downstream. This is the safety net that makes refactoring a machine's data shape safe: delete something still in use and the compiler stops you at the edit, not the user at runtime with a mystery failure.

**Design notes.** compile-time removal guard; the diagnostic names the dependent site; part of the object-removal safety story (§4.2).

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.19.json`.

title: sized ints int8..256 / uint8..256 (W6.2)
body:
Fixed-width integers from 8 to 256 bits, signed and unsigned — the types you need when a machine models hardware, a wire protocol, or anything where the bit-width is part of the contract. Declaring `uint8` means the value provably lives in 0..255, which the checker can reason about directly, instead of a 53-bit int you merely promise to keep small. Width becomes a fact the tooling can enforce rather than a comment.

**Design notes.** int8..int256 / uint8..uint256; the bounded widths sit in the SMT/large-finite band; overflow per W6.8; bitwise ops (W6.36) are restricted to exactly these types.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.1.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.2.json`.

title: ADT declarations + payloads (W6.20)
body:
Algebraic data types — named sum types whose variants carry typed payloads, like `Shape = Circle(r) | Rect(w, h)` — the single most important modeling tool for "this is one of several shapes of thing, each with its own data." Combined with exhaustive `case`, ADTs are how FSL makes illegal states unrepresentable and forces every variant to be handled, so adding a case later can't silently fall through.

**Design notes.** sum-type declarations with per-variant typed payloads; construct + pattern-match (W6.41); exhaustiveness-checked; value equality.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.20.json`.

title: recursive-ADT rich-gate (W6.21)
body:
Recursive ADTs — a tree, a linked list, a JSON value defined in terms of itself — admitted through a "rich gate" that allows them only where the machinery (equality, serialization, finiteness) can still cope. Recursion is what lets you model genuinely nested data; the gate is what keeps that power from silently breaking replay or blowing past the finite band without anyone noticing until a proof won't terminate.

**Design notes.** self-referential ADT definitions behind a capability gate; interacts with acyclic-data (W6.44), serialization, and the finite band.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.20.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.21.json`.

title: option<T> (W6.22)
body:
`option<T>` — a value that is either `some(x)` or `none` — the type that replaces null-checks with something the compiler forces you to unwrap. It is what `get` returns on a container miss, so "the key might not be there" becomes a fact in the type instead of a null-pointer waiting to happen. Pound for pound the cheapest defect-prevention type in the language.

**Design notes.** option<T> = some(x) | none; the return of get/find (W6.43); unwrap via case (W6.41); interacts with T? (W6.25).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.20.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.22.json`.

title: any + sound narrow-only via case (W6.23)
body:
An `any` escape hatch for when a value's type genuinely isn't known ahead of time — but narrowing out of `any` is only allowed soundly, through a `case` that checks, never by a bare cast. So `any` gives you the flexibility to handle genuinely dynamic data without giving up the guarantee that once you've used a value at a type, it really was that type.

**Design notes.** `any` top type; sound narrowing only via checked case (W6.41); no unchecked downcast; keeps the type system sound despite the escape hatch.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.23.json`.

title: type aliases (W6.24)
body:
Named aliases for types — `type Celsius = float` — so a complex or meaningful type gets one readable name used everywhere, and changing the underlying definition changes it in one place. Aliases are documentation the compiler keeps honest: `Celsius` and `Fahrenheit` read as different intentions even when both are float underneath.

**Design notes.** transparent type aliases; no new nominal identity (structural); improves readability and single-point-of-change.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.24.json`.

title: T? nullability (W6.25)
body:
`T?` as concise syntax for "a T or null," for the pragmatic cases where an optional field reads more naturally as a nullable than as a full `option<T>`. It keeps the null/undefined distinction honest while giving a lightweight spelling for the very common "might be absent" shape, and the checker still forces you to handle the null branch before you use the value.

**Design notes.** `T?` nullable type sugar; interacts with null/undefined (W6.12) and option (W6.22); checker enforces the null-handling branch.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.22.

Source: MP§4.2. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `data-types`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.25.json`.

title: fn types uncurried, inline vs first-class (W6.26)
body:
Function types as real types — uncurried, with both inline (lambda) and first-class (named, passed-around) forms — so a machine can take a strategy as a value and call it. This is the foundation of the State and Strategy patterns in FSL: behavior becomes data you can store in a prop or a val and swap at runtime, instead of structure baked into the graph at authoring time.

**Design notes.** uncurried fn types; inline lambdas + first-class fn values; the basis for fn-typed props (W6.29) and vals (W6.30); defunctionalized for identity (W6.27).

Source: MP§4.4. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `fn-slots`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.26.json`.

title: defunctionalization tag = normalized-AST hash (W6.27)
body:
Every function value gets a stable identity — a defunctionalization tag that is the hash of its normalized AST — so two functions that are structurally the same compare equal, and a function can be serialized, replayed, and named in a certificate. Without this, function-valued state couldn't be saved or compared at all; with it, behavior-as-data becomes as durable as any other value. The normalization is pinned forever, which is why it's an irreversibles item.

**Design notes.** defunctionalization tag = hash of the normalized AST (irreversibles #12 — spec the normalization at the lambda slice); enables equality, serialization, and replay of fn values; the normalization is pinned.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.26.

Source: MP§4.4. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `fn-slots`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.27.json`.

title: intensional equality (W6.28)
body:
Functions compare by intensional equality — same normalized structure means equal — rather than by reference identity, which wouldn't survive serialization or a second implementation. This is what makes "is this the same strategy as before" a meaningful, portable question instead of a pointer comparison that happens to be true only within one process on one host.

**Design notes.** intensional (structural) equality over the defunctionalization tag (W6.27); reference-independent; survives serialize/replay and the second implementation.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.27.

Source: MP§4.4. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `fn-slots`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.28.json`.

title: function-typed prop (State pattern, per-state pin) (W6.29)
body:
A state can pin a function-typed prop — behavior attached per state — which is the State pattern made native: entering a state selects an implementation, and the machine's behavior changes with its state without a pile of mode-checking conditionals. The classic "the object behaves differently depending on which mode it's in" modeled as exactly what it is, a function that comes with the state.

**Design notes.** function-typed property, pinnable per state; the State pattern; backed by fn types (W6.26) and their portable identity (W6.27); fsl#1026/#1027 lineage.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.26.

Source: MP§4.4. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `fn-slots`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.29.json`.

title: longint — arbitrary precision (W6.3)
body:
An arbitrary-precision integer for when 53 bits isn't enough and you refuse to lose a digit — big factorials, cryptographic-scale counters, exact combinatorics. It trades the provable finiteness of the sized ints for unbounded range, so by design it sits outside the model-checkable band; you reach for it precisely when correctness of the number matters more than decidability of the machine.

**Design notes.** arbitrary-precision (BigInt-backed); `L` literal suffix; not in the finite band; promotion into and out of it is explicit only.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.3.json`.

title: function-typed val (Strategy) (W6.30)
body:
A function-typed val — behavior stored in the machine's extended state as a value you can set and swap — which is the Strategy pattern: the algorithm a machine uses becomes runtime-configurable data rather than hard-coded structure. Combined with the portable identity of functions, the chosen strategy serializes and replays with the rest of the state, so a machine's behavior travels with its snapshot.

**Design notes.** function-typed val (extended state); the Strategy pattern; swappable at runtime; serializes via the defunctionalization tag (W6.27).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.26.

Source: MP§4.4. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `fn-slots`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.30.json`.

title: stream type: construct/draw/serialize/finite-eligibility (W6.31)
body:
A stream type — a lazily-produced sequence you can construct, draw from, and serialize — for randomness sources, input feeds, and generated sequences, with explicit rules for when a stream counts as finite and so keeps the machine checkable. Streams are how FSL handles "a source of values over time" without giving up replay: draw from the same seeded stream, get the same sequence, every time and everywhere.

**Design notes.** stream type with construct/draw/serialize; finite-eligibility rules for the checkable band; supplies the random-stream argument to rand (W6.52); §4.6.

Source: MP§4.6. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `streams`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.31.json`.

title: arithmetic + div/mod/rem (Erlang div) (W6.32)
body:
The arithmetic operators, with integer division split the way that actually prevents bugs: separate `div`/`mod`/`rem` following the Erlang convention, so the sign behavior of remainder is defined and predictable instead of the C/JavaScript coin-flip that differs by language. When a machine computes a wrapped index or a schedule slot, "what does -7 mod 3 give" has exactly one answer here, not one per host.

**Design notes.** +,-,*, integer div/mod/rem per Erlang semantics (sign-defined); result typing via promotion (W6.7); division by zero is an error, never NaN/Infinity.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.32.json`.

title: comparisons, cross-type error, total compare (W6.33)
body:
Comparisons are total — every pair of same-type values orders definitively, with NaN handled explicitly — and comparing across incompatible types is a compile error, not a silent `false`. JavaScript's coercing equality is a decade of bugs in one operator; FSL refuses the coercions and makes "is this less than that" a question with a real, checkable answer instead of a surprise that depends on which operand came first.

**Design notes.** total order per type; cross-type comparison is a type error (no coercion); explicit NaN handling; underpins case guards and container sort.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.33.json`.

title: boolean zoo (nand/xor/implies + andalso/orelse) (W6.34)
body:
The full set of boolean operators — not just and/or/not but nand, xor, and implies, plus short-circuiting andalso/orelse — because machine guards are logic, and having `implies` as a first-class operator lets a contract read like the proposition it actually is instead of a de Morgan puzzle you re-derive every time. The short-circuit forms give you control over evaluation order when a guard has a side condition or an expensive test.

**Design notes.** and/or/not/nand/xor/implies + short-circuit andalso/orelse; total boolean algebra; feeds guards (W6.61), contracts (W6.62-.64), and quantifiers (W6.42).

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.34.json`.

title: in membership operator (W6.35)
body:
An `in` operator for membership testing against sets, ranges, and containers — `x in #[a b c]`, `n in 0..9` — so the single most common guard shape reads in one glance instead of a chain of ORs. Small on its own, but it is the difference between a guard a reviewer can read at a glance and one they have to mentally parse into a truth table.

**Design notes.** `in` over sets/bags/ranges/containers; total; desugars to the container membership protocol (W6.43).

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.35.json`.

title: bitwise on sized ints only (W6.36)
body:
Bitwise operators (and/or/xor/not, shifts) restricted to sized integers only — because bit-twiddling on a 53-bit float-backed int is exactly where silent corruption lives. By requiring a declared width, FSL makes bit manipulation mean something precise, and refuses it on the types where it wouldn't. If you want to shift bits, you first have to say how many bits there are.

**Design notes.** bitwise ops only on sized ints (W6.2); shifts respect the declared width; a type error on unsized/float operands.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.2.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.36.json`.

title: ++ concat and |> pipe (W6.37)
body:
Concatenation (`++`) for joining sequences and strings, and the pipe operator (`|>`) for threading a value through a chain of transforms left to right. The pipe is what turns a nested pile of function calls into a readable pipeline — `x |> f |> g` instead of `g(f(x))` — which matters enormously once the expression language and stdlib give you functions actually worth chaining.

**Design notes.** `++` sequence/string concat; `|>` left-to-right application; precedence pinned in W6.38; pairs with the HOFs (W6.43).

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.37.json`.

title: precedence table (grammar-pinned) (W6.38)
body:
One authoritative operator-precedence table, pinned in the grammar, so `a + b * c` and every other mixed expression parse the same way forever and on every tool that reads FSL. Precedence that drifts between a parser and a formatter is a silent meaning-change — the worst kind, because the source looks unchanged — so nailing it in the grammar and fuzzing it with the dragon makes the parse a fact rather than a per-implementation opinion.

**Design notes.** full precedence/associativity table in the pinned grammar (C3); dragon-fuzzed; formatter and the second implementation conform to it; covers arithmetic/comparison/boolean/bitwise/pipe/concat.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.32, W6.34.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.38.json`.

title: if/then/else expression (W6.39)
body:
`if/then/else` as an expression that yields a value, not a statement that performs an effect — so a guard or an assignment can branch inline and every branch is required to produce a value of the same type. This is the functional shape that keeps FSL expressions total and typed, instead of leaking the host language's statement-oriented control flow into machine logic where it can't be reasoned about.

**Design notes.** expression-form if/then/else; both branches required, unified result type; totality-preserving.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.39.json`.

title: float/double + IEEE + isnan/isinf/isfinite (W6.4)
body:
IEEE-754 floating point for the cases that genuinely want it — physics, graphics, approximate measurement — with the sharp edges made first-class rather than hidden. `isnan`, `isinf`, and `isfinite` are language builtins, because in FSL a NaN sneaking into a guard should be a question you can explicitly ask, not a silent surprise that makes a transition fire wrong. Float is explicit, so nobody inherits binary drift by accident when they wanted exact.

**Design notes.** float (32) / double (64) IEEE-754; isnan/isinf/isfinite predicates; no silent promotion from int (W6.7); comparisons total per W6.33.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.4.json`.

title: let-in binding (W6.40)
body:
`let x = ... in ...` for naming a subexpression locally without reaching for mutable state, so a complex guard or assignment can introduce a readable intermediate name that exists only for that expression. Pure, scoped, and side-effect-free, which is what keeps the expression analyzable by the checker instead of becoming a little imperative program hiding inside a transition.

**Design notes.** `let`-bindings scoped to the `in` body; immutable; by-value; no mutation escapes the expression.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.40.json`.

title: case: patterns, when, exhaustiveness + else (W6.41)
body:
A real `case` with full structural patterns, `when` guards, and — the part that earns its keep — exhaustiveness checking with an explicit `else`. The compiler proves you have handled every possibility of an enum or ADT, so adding a variant later turns every unhandled `case` into a compile error pointing at exactly what you forgot. This is the single biggest defect-prevention feature in the whole expression language.

**Design notes.** structural patterns + `when` guards; exhaustiveness over enums (W6.11) and ADTs (W6.20); explicit `else`; a non-exhaustive case without `else` is a compile error.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.11.

Source: MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.41.json`.

title: forall/exists bounded quantifiers (W6.42)
body:
Bounded universal and existential quantifiers — `forall x in coll: P(x)`, `exists x in coll: P(x)` — so a guard or contract can say "every pending order is under budget" directly, as a proposition, instead of a hand-rolled fold nobody trusts. Because the domain is bounded the quantifier stays computable and the checker can reason about it, which makes it the natural bridge from ordinary expressions to the temporal properties of the proofs era.

**Design notes.** bounded forall/exists over finite containers/ranges; total; desugars to all/any over the container protocol (W6.43); a precursor to the §17 temporal property language.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.43.

Source: MP§6, MP§13. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `expressions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.42.json`.

title: container protocol: bracket-throws, get→option, HOFs, no foreach (W6.43)
body:
One uniform protocol every container answers to: bracket indexing throws on a miss (fail loud), `get` returns an option instead (fail safe — your choice at the call site), and a full set of higher-order functions (map, filter, foldl/foldr, all, any, find, count, sort, flat_map) with deliberately no `foreach`, because side-effecting iteration is precisely the thing FSL is designing out. This is how you compute over data without writing a loop that mutates something behind your back.

**Design notes.** bracket-access throws / `get`->option (W6.22); HOFs map/filter/foldl/foldr/all/any/find/count/sort/flat_map; no foreach; the concrete container types it abstracts over land in v8 (W6.13-.18).

Source: MP§6, MP§13. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `totality`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.43.json`.

title: by-value capture + acyclic-data enforcement (W6.44)
body:
When an expression or lambda captures data, it captures by value, and the language enforces that captured data is acyclic — no shared mutable references, no reference cycles that make equality and serialization undecidable. This is what keeps FSL values comparable, hashable, and replayable: a captured value is a snapshot, not a live pointer into somebody else's state that changes under you between two reads.

**Design notes.** by-value capture; acyclic-data enforcement (compile/runtime guard); underpins intensional equality (W6.28), serialization, and the defunctionalization hash (W6.27).

Source: MP§13. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `totality`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.44.json`.

title: microstep bound (100k default, config) (W6.45)
body:
A hard ceiling on how many microsteps a single macrostep may take — 100k by default, configurable — so a machine that would otherwise spin forever in eventless transitions faults loudly instead of hanging the host process. This is FSL's totality backstop: it turns "this might not terminate" from an undecidable worry into a bounded, observable error you can tune per machine, which is the pragmatic price of letting the graph loop at all.

**Design notes.** microstep counter with a configurable bound (default 100k); exceeding it raises a fault at the current stable point; the guardrail behind the eventless/else cascade (W6.90) and the RTC internal-queue drain (W6.73).

Source: MP§13. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `totality`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.45.json`.

title: rounding/clamp/lerp family (W6.46)
body:
The everyday numeric-shaping functions — round/floor/ceil/trunc, clamp to a range, lerp between two values — as pinned builtins, so a machine that eases an animation or clamps a sensor reading gets the identical result on every host. These are the functions people reimplement slightly wrong in every codebase; blessing and specifying them removes a whole category of off-by-a-rounding-mode bug that otherwise only shows up on one platform.

**Design notes.** round/floor/ceil/trunc (rounding mode explicit), clamp, lerp; pinned semantics (irreversibles #13 blessed infra); cross-host identical.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.46.json`.

title: exp/log family (W6.47)
body:
Exponential and logarithm builtins (exp, log, log2, log10, pow) pinned to defined semantics, for machines doing growth, decay, or scaling math. Transcendental functions are exactly where hosts quietly disagree in the last bit; FSL specifies them so results are portable rather than a platform lottery you only lose in production on the one machine that rounds the other way.

**Design notes.** exp/log/log2/log10/pow; pinned semantics; documented precision posture for cross-host agreement.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.47.json`.

title: trig — degrees default + radians (W6.48)
body:
Trigonometry with degrees as the default and radians available explicitly — because most people reaching for sin/cos in a state machine are thinking in degrees, and silently defaulting to radians is a reliable source of "why is my angle 57 times too big" bugs. Both are present; the default simply matches how humans actually specify angles instead of how the math library happens to.

**Design notes.** sin/cos/tan/asin/acos/atan/atan2 and friends; degrees default, radians variants explicit; pinned semantics.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.48.json`.

title: gcd/lcm/factorial/comb/perm (W6.49)
body:
The integer-combinatorics builtins — gcd, lcm, factorial, combinations, permutations — for machines doing scheduling, counting, or number-theory logic without hand-rolling the recursion and getting the base case wrong. Blessed, tested, and exact on the integer types, so a scheduling machine that computes how many arrangements exist gets a right answer rather than a plausible one.

**Design notes.** gcd/lcm/factorial/comb/perm over integer types; exact; factorial/comb/perm may need longint (W6.3) to hold their range.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.49.json`.

title: decimal(p,s) — exact base-10 numerics (W6.5)
body:
An exact decimal number type — the number type for money and measurement. Binary floats drift (0.1 + 0.2 is not 0.3), which is why financial code in float languages is its own genre of bug. FSL's decimal makes 19.99 plus tax exact by construction, and a bounded decimal(p,s) — p digits, s after the point — stays inside the provable tier, so machines that move money can still be model-checked rather than merely tested.

**Design notes.** Fixed-point over BigInt scaled by 10^s; banker's (half-even) rounding default with modes enumerable; promotion into decimal is explicit only, never silent from float; bounded form sits in the large-finite/SMT band; overflow errors like the sized ints, with opt-in saturating.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.1; blocks W6.52.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.5.json`.

title: popcount/clz/ctz/rotl/rotr (W6.50)
body:
The bit-population and bit-rotation intrinsics — popcount, count-leading-zeros, count-trailing-zeros, rotate-left/right — for machines modeling low-level hardware or doing bitset tricks. Each maps to a single CPU instruction in a compiled host, so having them as blessed builtins means the codegen can emit the fast instruction instead of an unrolled loop that computes the same thing slowly.

**Design notes.** popcount/clz/ctz/rotl/rotr on sized ints (W6.2); width-aware; lower to native intrinsics in compiled targets.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.2.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.50.json`.

title: stats family (W6.51)
body:
A basic statistics kit — mean, median, variance, stddev, min/max, sum — over collections, so a machine that summarizes a window of readings doesn't reimplement statistics and quietly pick the wrong variance denominator. Pinned, so "the average" and "the standard deviation" mean the same thing everywhere and the sample-versus-population choice is specified rather than accidental.

**Design notes.** mean/median/variance/stddev/min/max/sum over numeric collections; sample-vs-population variance specified; feeds windowed aggregates (W7.33).

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.51.json`.

title: rand family + optional stream + finite over-approximation (W6.52)
body:
Randomness done so it stays replayable: the rand family takes an optional explicit random stream, so a machine's "random" choice is a pure function of a seed you control — which is exactly what makes seeded walks and deterministic replay possible at all. For the checker, randomness is finitely over-approximated, so a machine that flips coins can still be reasoned about as a finite object rather than becoming unanalyzable the moment it rolls a die.

**Design notes.** rand family with an optional stream argument (W6.31); seed-driven and replayable (feeds W6.100); finite over-approximation for the checkable band; a default stream when none is supplied.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.52.json`.

title: math constants + per-type MIN/MAX/EPSILON (W6.53)
body:
The blessed constants — pi, e, tau — plus per-type MIN/MAX/EPSILON, so a machine can ask "what is the largest int8" or "the float epsilon" as a named value instead of a magic literal it might transcribe wrong. Boundary constants off by one are a classic overflow bug; naming them and deriving them per type removes the guesswork and lets the checker use them too.

**Design notes.** pi/e/tau and friends; per-type MIN/MAX/EPSILON; compile-time constants; pairs with overflow (W6.8) and promotion (W6.7).

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.53.json`.

title: encoding: base64/base64url/hex/percent (W6.54)
body:
The standard encodings — base64, base64url, hex, percent/URL — as pinned builtins, so a machine that emits or ingests encoded data agrees byte-for-byte with the rest of the world and with itself across hosts. Encoding is deceptively full of variant edge cases (padding rules, url-safe alphabets); blessing one specified implementation kills the interop bugs that otherwise appear only when two systems that each "do base64" try to talk.

**Design notes.** base64/base64url/hex/percent encode+decode; pinned alphabets/padding; operate over bytearray (W6.10); part of the blessed contract-coupled infra.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.10.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.54.json`.

title: pinned hash builtin (W6.55)
body:
A single blessed hash function pinned to an exact algorithm (named in the conformance vectors), so a machine that hashes agrees with every other implementation forever. This is contract-coupled infrastructure — the same pinning discipline as the canonical source hash — because a hash that varies by host is worse than no hash at all: it looks like it works right up until two systems compute different digests for the same bytes.

**Design notes.** pinned hash builtin, algorithm named in the vectors; over bytearray/string; contract-coupled (sibling of C1, W6.99); cross-host identical.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.10.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.55.json`.

title: zstd+lz4, pinned encoder, url-safe compose (W6.56)
body:
Blessed compression — zstd and lz4 — with a pinned encoder so a compressed blob round-trips identically everywhere, plus a url-safe composition so compressed data can ride inside a link (the viewer/permalink use case). Pinning the encoder, not just the decoder, means outputs are reproducible, which is what lets you hash and diff compressed artifacts instead of getting a different blob every time you compress the same input.

**Design notes.** zstd + lz4, pinned encoder for reproducible output; url-safe compose path; over bytearray (W6.10); pairs with the encodings (W6.54).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.10.

Source: MP§7. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `stdlib`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.56.json`.

title: portable string ops + negative index + grapheme + / byte view (W6.57)
body:
A portable, fully-specified set of string operations that behave identically on every host — including negative indexing from the end, grapheme-aware concatenation so combining characters join correctly, and a deliberate byte view for when you need the underlying bytes. The whole point is that string code written once gives the same answer everywhere, which is emphatically not true of the host languages FSL compiles down to.

**Design notes.** portable op set (slice/index/find/split/etc.); negative indices; grapheme-cluster-correct `+`; byte-view bridge to bytearray (W6.10); every op specified for cross-host identity.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.9.

Source: MP§8. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `strings`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.57.json`.

title: normalize/case pinned to shipped Unicode (W6.58)
body:
Case-folding and normalization (NFC/NFD and friends) pinned to a specific shipped Unicode version, so "uppercase this" and "are these two strings equal after normalization" give the same answer on every host and never drift when a runtime updates its Unicode tables underneath you. Unpinned Unicode is a silent cross-host correctness bug — the same machine disagreeing with itself on two computers — and this nails it shut.

**Design notes.** normalize + case ops pinned to a bundled Unicode version, not the host's; cross-host determinism now, full cross-host conformance certification later (v11); interacts with the `unicode: N` attribute (W6.59).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.9.

Source: MP§8. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `strings`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.58.json`.

title: unicode: N attribute + finite hard-match (W6.59)
body:
A machine can declare `unicode: N` to fix exactly which Unicode version its string semantics use and to hard-match a finite alphabet, turning "strings over these characters" into a closed, checkable set. This is what lets a machine that processes text still make finiteness claims, and what keeps its meaning stable across the decades Unicode will keep changing out from under it.

**Design notes.** `unicode: N` machine attribute pins the Unicode version; enables finite hard-match over a declared alphabet; feeds the finite band and the charset/unicode-rampage umbrella (fsl#1199/#1379).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.9.

Source: MP§8. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `strings`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.59.json`.

title: bounded scalar ranges (int 0..3) (W6.6)
body:
Any integer type can be pinned to an explicit range — `int 0..3` — turning a value into a small closed set the checker can enumerate. This is the difference between "a number I trust stays in range" and "a number the tool proves stays in range," and it is what lets a machine's data be model-checked rather than merely tested. It is also the honest way to model a dial with four positions instead of a full integer you swear never leaves 0..3.

**Design notes.** range annotation on any int type; small ranges land in the enumerable/SMT band; hitting a bound triggers overflow handling per W6.8 (error or opt-in saturating).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.1.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.6.json`.

title: assign whole+slot, sequential, old (W6.60)
body:
The assignment statement — set a whole val or a slot within it, in sequence, with `old` available to name a slot's pre-transition value. This is how a machine mutates its extended state deliberately and observably: assignments are journaled so they can roll back cleanly on a contract failure, and `old` is what lets an `ensure` clause compare after to before. Mutation with a memory and an undo, not a free-for-all.

**Design notes.** whole-val + slot assignment, sequential semantics; `old` binds the pre-macrostep value; journaled for rollback (W6.69); the assign grammar itself is unfiled (carrier is fsl#1355-adjacent).

Source: MP§9. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `mutation`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.60.json`.

title: where guards on transitions (W6.61)
body:
`where` clauses attach a boolean guard to a transition, so an edge only fires when its condition holds — the workhorse of every data-dependent state machine. It reads as "take this arrow where balance > 0," making the guard part of the transition's identity, and it composes with the resolution order so a guarded and an unguarded edge on the same action have a defined, non-surprising precedence. This is the first place the expression language reaches out and touches the graph itself.

**Design notes.** `where <expr>` guard on transitions; the expression is a total boolean (W6.34); participates in edge resolution order (W6.89); a false guard makes the edge ineligible, not an error.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.34.

Source: MP§9, MP§6. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `guards`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.61.json`.

title: require (precondition) (W6.62)
body:
`require` — a precondition contract on a transition or action: the condition must hold on entry or the transition is a violation. This is design-by-contract's first pillar in FSL, turning "the caller must ensure X" from a hopeful comment into a checked obligation. Under a sealed or proven build it can compile out entirely, once the checker has proven it always holds, so the guarantee costs nothing in production.

**Design notes.** `require <expr>` precondition (§10); boolean expression (W6.34); violation semantics per W6.65; compiles out under sealed/proven when discharged.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.34.

Source: MP§10. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `contracts`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.62.json`.

title: ensure + old (postcondition) (W6.63)
body:
`ensure` — a postcondition contract that must hold after a transition, with `old` to reference the pre-transition state so you can say "the balance decreased by exactly the amount withdrawn." Postconditions are how a machine states what a transition guarantees, and `old` is what makes those guarantees relational rather than merely point-in-time snapshots. The second pillar of design-by-contract.

**Design notes.** `ensure <expr>` postcondition with `old(slot)` (§10); needs the assign/journal pre-state (W6.60); violation per W6.65.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.60.

Source: MP§10. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `contracts`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.63.json`.

title: violation model: obligation + debug assert + compile-out (W6.65)
body:
The unified model for what a contract violation is and does: at heart an obligation, checked as a debug assertion during development, and compiled out entirely under a sealed or proven build once the checker has discharged it. This is what lets contracts be everywhere in development without paying for them in production — you write guarantees liberally, and proof rather than faith decides which ones still cost runtime.

**Design notes.** violation = obligation; runtime debug-assert tier; compile-out under sealed/proven once the checker discharges it; the shared semantics for require/ensure/invariant (W6.62-.64).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.62, W6.63, W6.64.

Source: MP§10, MP§11. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `violation-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.65.json`.

title: last_error/error_count + Error.kind enum (W6.66)
body:
A queryable error surface — `last_error`, `error_count`, and an `Error.kind` enum — so a machine and its host can inspect what went wrong as structured data rather than a string to grep. Making errors first-class values with a typed kind is what lets error-handling logic branch reliably, and what feeds the error tape and the recovery transitions instead of everyone reinventing error plumbing.

**Design notes.** last_error/error_count getters; Error.kind enum (§11); structured errors, not strings; feeds error states (W6.70) and the error tape (W6.67).

Source: MP§11. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `error-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.66.json`.

title: error tape (regenerated class) (W6.67)
body:
The error tape — a recorded stream of the errors a machine has produced, in the same tape family as input and output — so a run's failures are a replayable, inspectable artifact rather than lost console output. This is the diagnostics substrate: a bug report can ship the error tape, and the whole run can be replayed to reproduce exactly how the machine arrived at the failure, which is the difference between a fixable bug and a shrug.

**Design notes.** error tape in the tape family (§11/§14); regenerated class; replayable; pairs with the output/input tapes (W7.9) and the era-4 flight recorder.

Source: MP§11, MP§14. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `error-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.67.json`.

title: rollback event on the bus + pre-error snapshot (W6.68)
body:
When a macrostep rolls back after a violation, it emits a rollback event carrying a snapshot of the pre-error state, so observers can see exactly what was attempted and undone. Rollback becomes an observable, first-class event rather than a silent revert — which is what makes contract failures debuggable, and what lets a supervisor or logger react to a failed transaction instead of never learning it happened.

**Design notes.** rollback event on the observation bus with a pre-error snapshot payload; fires on macrostep reverse-replay (W6.69); consumed through the hook registry (W6.77).

Source: MP§11. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `error-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.68.json`.

title: undo-journal rollback implementation (with #756) (W6.69)
body:
The actual rollback mechanism: an undo journal that records each assignment so a failed macrostep can be reversed exactly, restoring the machine to its last stable state. This is what makes FSL's transactional guarantee real — a macrostep either commits wholly or leaves no trace — and it rides the same columnar-val journal as the performance keystone, so correctness and speed share one implementation instead of two that drift apart.

**Design notes.** undo-log journal; reverse-replay on violation to the last stable state; shares the columnar-val journal (Tjssm-756); the mechanism behind the rollback event (W6.68).

**Dependencies.** Depends on Tjssm-756, W6.68.

Source: MP§11, MP§28. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `error-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.69.json`.

title: numeric promotion rules (W6.7)
body:
The exact, spelled-out rules for when one numeric type becomes another — and, crucially, that it never happens silently across the lines that matter (int to float, bounded to unbounded). Implicit numeric promotion is a classic bug factory; FSL makes the safe widenings automatic and the lossy conversions require you to say so, so a machine can't quietly turn an exact decimal into a drifting float and only notice three states later.

**Design notes.** a total, spelled promotion lattice; widening within a kind may be implicit; int->float and bounded->unbounded are explicit-only; feeds the type checker's arithmetic result typing.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.1.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.7.json`.

title: error states — state X: { error; } (W6.70)
body:
Error states — states explicitly marked `error` — so a machine can model "something went wrong and we are now in a failure state" as a real, queryable part of the graph. Marking a state as an error target lets the safety checker verify whether error states are reachable (usually you want to prove they aren't), and lets tooling and rendering treat failure distinctly from ordinary states.

**Design notes.** `state X: { error; }` error-state marking (§11); queryable; feeds the safety checker's error-target reachability (W6.95) and rendering/kinds (W6.92).

Source: MP§11. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `error-model`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.70.json`.

title: priority + doc-order selection (W6.72)
body:
The rule for which enabled transition is selected when several compete: explicit priority first, then document order as the deterministic tiebreak. This is what makes a machine's choice among simultaneously-enabled transitions predictable and reproducible instead of implementation-defined — the same source picks the same transition on every host, which is a precondition for replay meaning anything.

**Design notes.** transition selection by explicit priority then document order; deterministic; part of the macrostep (W6.71); interacts with the edge resolution order (W6.89).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.71.

Source: MP§12. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `rtc`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.72.json`.

title: internal-event FIFO drain (W6.73)
body:
