Internal events (raised by the machine itself during a macrostep) are queued and drained first-in-first-out before the macrostep completes, so a cascade of self-triggered transitions runs in a defined order to a stable point. FIFO drain is what makes run-to-completion actually complete predictably; the microstep bound is what stops an ill-formed cascade from draining forever and hanging the host.

**Design notes.** internal-event FIFO queue drained within the macrostep (W6.71); bounded by the microstep limit (W6.45); the ordering is part of the C2 conformance surface.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.71.

Source: MP§12. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `rtc`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.73.json`.

title: before-macrostep / at-stable / post-commit hook points (W6.74)
body:
The three macrostep-level hook points — before the macrostep, at the stable point, after commit — so host code can observe the machine at exactly the moments that matter without wrapping every transition. These are the coarse-grained observation seams for logging, metrics, and integration, defined relative to the RTC pipeline so their timing is precise, portable, and the same on every host.

**Design notes.** before-macrostep / at-stable / post-commit hook points on the RTC pipeline (W6.71); registered via the uniform registry (W6.77); timing pinned by C2.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.71.

Source: MP§12. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `hooks`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.74.json`.

title: named handlers (handler h = {…}) (W6.75)
body:
Named handlers — `handler h = {…}` — reusable hook bodies you define once and attach in multiple places, instead of copy-pasting an inline hook onto every transition that needs it. This is what turns hooking from per-edge boilerplate into composable, named behavior, and named handlers are what a machine can later require or declare as part of its public interface.

**Design notes.** `handler h = {…}` named, reusable hook bodies; attachable at multiple points; the basis for required/declared hooks (W7.6); §12/§14.

Source: MP§12, MP§14. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `hooks`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.75.json`.

title: uniform observational registry (kind, target, phase) (W6.77)
body:
One uniform registry that every hook and observer flows through, keyed by (kind, target, phase) — so instead of a dozen bespoke hook mechanisms there is a single indexed system that construct-hooks, transition-hooks, boundary-hooks, and lifecycle-hooks all register into. This is the architectural spine of the entire hook story: it is what makes hooks introspectable, closeable, and uniform, and it is where the old scattered hook APIs finally converge.

**Design notes.** uniform observational registry keyed by (kind, target, phase); every hook kind registers here; enables introspection (W6.78) and closed-enforcement (W6.79); the §12/§14 convergence point that supersedes the on_* grammar cluster.

Source: MP§12, MP§14. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `hooks`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.77.json`.

title: overflow = error + opt-in saturating (W6.8)
body:
When a sized or bounded integer would overflow, the default is a loud error — not the silent wraparound that turns 255+1 into 0 and ships a security bug. If wraparound or clamping is genuinely what you want, you opt into saturating arithmetic explicitly. Safe by default, dangerous only on request — the opposite of the C tradition where overflow is silent and correctness is the programmer's unpaid job.

**Design notes.** overflow -> error by default; opt-in saturating (clamp-at-bound) mode; applies to sized ints (W6.2) and bounded ranges (W6.6).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.1.

Source: MP§4.1. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `numeric-tower`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.8.json`.

title: &group:[...] first-class groups, overlap+nest, DAG check (W6.80)
body:
First-class groups — `&mygroup: [a b c]` — that name a set of states and may overlap and nest, with a DAG check that forbids illegal cyclic containment. Groups are how FSL expresses hierarchy and cross-cutting sets (all the "error" states, all the "logged-in" states) without forcing everything into a strict tree, and they are the foundation the entire §19 cluster — cascade, boundary hooks, history, spread — builds on top of.

**Design notes.** `&group:[...]` first-class groups; overlap + nesting allowed; a containment DAG check rejects cycles; the base that the other group features (W6.81-.86) extend.

Source: MP§19. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `groups`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.80.json`.

title: nest &c vs spread ...&c (W6.81)
body:
Two distinct ways to bring one group into another: nesting (`&c` — c becomes a sub-group) versus spreading (`...&c` — c's members are pulled in flat). The distinction matters because it decides whether the inner group's boundary and history survive, and having both spellings lets you compose groups the way the model actually calls for instead of fighting a single fixed containment rule.

**Design notes.** `&c` nest (preserves inner group identity/boundary) vs `...&c` spread (flattens members); different boundary/history/cascade consequences; fsl#453/#72/#142 spread cluster.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.80.

Source: MP§19. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `groups`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.81.json`.

title: group boundary hooks (W6.82)
body:
Hooks that fire when execution crosses a group's boundary — entering or leaving the set of states the group names — so cross-cutting concerns (start a timer whenever we enter any "active" state, log every exit from the "critical" region) attach to the group instead of being copied onto every member transition. Define the behavior once on the boundary, not N times on the edges, and it can't drift out of sync as members change.

**Design notes.** enter/leave-group boundary hook points; fire on crossing the group membership boundary; interact with nest vs spread (W6.81); registered through the uniform observational registry (W6.77).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.80.

Source: MP§19. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `groups`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.82.json`.

title: membership queries in(&g)/groupsOf/statesIn (W6.83)
body:
Runtime queries over group membership — `in(&g)` (is the current state in this group), `groupsOf(s)` (which groups contain s), `statesIn(&g)` (the group's members) — so guards and logic can ask about hierarchy directly. `in(&g)` in particular becomes a guard predicate, letting a transition depend on "are we anywhere in the active region," and it later feeds the temporal properties of the proofs era.

**Design notes.** in(&g)/groupsOf/statesIn query API; in(&g) usable as a guard predicate (W6.61); feeds era-3 properties; fsl#1343 in-state lineage.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.80.

Source: MP§19. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `groups`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.83.json`.

title: per-group history (W6.85)
body:
Per-group history — a group can remember which of its members was last active, so re-entering the group returns you there (the history-pseudostate idea, scoped to a group). This is what lets a "paused" region resume where it left off instead of resetting to the start, modeled cleanly at the group level rather than bolted onto individual states with hand-tracked bookkeeping.

**Design notes.** per-group history (shallow/deep per config); re-entry restores the last-active member; interacts with nest vs spread (W6.81) and the statechart history pseudostate (W7.30).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.80.

Source: MP§19. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `groups`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.85.json`.

title: repeat-state declaration merge (#454) (W6.86)
body:
Today, declaring the same state in two blocks is a hard error, which blocks the natural workflow where a state picks up properties from several places — a spread here, a styling block there. v8 makes repeated declarations merge like CSS: saying the same thing twice is harmless, saying two conflicting things is a loud error that names both locations. This is what lets groups, spreads, and per-state styling compose instead of colliding.

**Design notes.** Per-state attribute maps union across declaration sites; same-key/same-value is idempotent; same-key/different-value at equal cascade tier is a compile error carrying both source spans; tier order theme < state:{} < kind < group-by-depth < state-specific; dragon: fuzz N declarations and assert order-independence of the merged result.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.80.

Source: MP§19, fsl#454. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `groups`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.86.json`.

title: multigraph action-name rule + ≤1 unlabeled edge (W6.87)
body:
The rules that make FSL a proper multigraph: multiple edges can connect the same two states as long as their action names disambiguate them, and at most one unlabeled edge is allowed between a pair, because more would be genuinely ambiguous. This is what lets a machine have several distinct transitions between the same two states without the graph becoming undecidable about which one a given action meant.

**Design notes.** multiple edges per state-pair keyed by action name; at most one unlabeled edge per ordered pair; further ambiguity is a compile error; underpins the resolution order (W6.89).

Source: MP§28. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `graph`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.87.json`.

title: resolution order: specific>wildcard-action>unlabeled>wildcard-source>rejection (W6.89)
body:
The single, total precedence that decides which transition fires when several could match: specific beats wildcard-action beats unlabeled beats wildcard-source beats rejection. This is the rule that makes wildcards, unlabeled edges, and specific transitions coexist predictably — without it, a machine with overlapping edges would be a guessing game about which arrow wins. It is the graph's definitive answer to "which transition applies."

**Design notes.** total resolution order specific > wildcard-action > unlabeled > wildcard-source > rejection; deterministic edge selection; consumes the multigraph rules (W6.87) and wildcards (W6.88).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.87, W6.88.

Source: MP§28. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `graph`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.89.json`.

title: string codepoint model + bounded len (W6.9)
body:
FSL strings are sequences of Unicode codepoints, not UTF-16 code units, so `length` and indexing mean the human-obvious thing instead of splitting an emoji in half — the bug that quietly plagues JavaScript string code everywhere. Strings can also carry a bounded length, which drops them into the finite band, so a machine working over a bounded-string alphabet stays model-checkable instead of exploding to infinity the moment text is involved.

**Design notes.** codepoint (scalar value) model, not UTF-16 units; optional bounded length -> finite band; grapheme-aware ops in W6.57; normalization pinned in W6.58.

Source: MP§4.1, MP§8. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `strings`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.9.json`.

title: editions + feature-gates + lint --future (W6.94)
body:
An editions mechanism (in the spirit of Rust's) plus feature-gates, so new language features can land on trunk turned off, ship dark, and get opted into per machine — and `lint --future` warns you about code that a coming edition will change. This is what lets FSL evolve on a single main branch without breaking existing machines: the breakage batches at era boundaries ride this mechanism, and authors migrate at their own pace instead of on a flag day.

**Design notes.** editions + feature-gate mechanism (§15); `lint --future` surfaces upcoming-edition changes; the vehicle for shipping waves dark on trunk (era-1 feature gates).

Source: MP§15. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `editions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.94.json`.

title: native safety checker: reachability + invariants + error-state targets (W6.95)
body:
The first verification engine that ships in FSL — a native, backend-free safety checker that proves reachability (can we get to this state), checks invariants hold across all reachable states, and flags whether error states are reachable. No SAT/SMT backends yet, just a built-in breadth-first search over the reachable configuration space, but this is the moment FSL stops merely running machines and starts proving things about them.

**Design notes.** native BFS safety checker (§17-safety); reachability, invariant-holds, and error-state target analysis; no external backends yet (those are era 3); consumes invariants (W6.64) and error states (W6.70).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.64, W6.70.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `safety-checker`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.95.json`.

title: M1 check verb + unsigned certificate record (W6.96)
body:
The `check` verb plus an unsigned certificate record — run the safety checker and emit a structured, machine-readable record of what was checked and what held, bound to the canonical source hash. This is milestone 1 of the trust stack: not signed yet, but the record format is the irreversible part, and everything the later signing, verify, and receipts machinery does is built on this record existing and binding to the right hash.

**Design notes.** `check` verb + unsigned certificate record (irreversibles #15); subject = canonical source hash (W6.99); runs the safety checker (W6.95); the record format is the irreversible piece, signing lands era 5; jssm twins #825/#623.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.99, W6.95.

Source: MP§17, era1/WP-1.8. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `m-track`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.96.json`.

title: M4-subset predicates over runs (W6.97)
body:
A built-in subset of run-predicates — `reached_state`, `in_config`, `is_legal_run`, `never_entered` — that evaluate over a recorded run (an M3 tape) without needing the full expression language. This is milestone 4's early half: it lets you ask concrete questions about what actually happened in a run right now, straight off the replay engine, while the full property language waits for the P2 expression language to mature.

**Design notes.** M4-subset predicates (reached_state/in_config/is_legal_run/never_entered) over M3 runs (Tjssm-827); no P2 dependency by design; the full property language is era 3; jssm twin #828.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Tjssm-827.

Source: MP§17, era1/WP-1.8. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `m-track`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.97.json`.

title: budget/--estimate/undecided tri-state (W6.98)
body:
The honesty layer for verification: a budget on how hard the checker will try, an `--estimate` of the cost before you commit to it, and a basic true/false/undecided tri-state result — because a checker that can't finish should say "undecided," not hang and not lie. This is what makes the safety checker usable in practice, and it sets the precedent that FSL's verification always tells you when it doesn't know rather than pretending it does.

**Design notes.** verification budget + `--estimate` + true/false/undecided tri-state; undecided is a first-class result; the honesty posture the whole proofs era inherits.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `m-track`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W6.98.json`.

title: sensor wiring live (W7.1)
body:
Sensors go live — external readings declared as typed, read-only, level-triggered inputs that a machine samples, with every read recorded to the input tape at commit so the run replays exactly. This is the moment a machine stops being a closed system and starts observing the world without giving up determinism: the recorded reads are the source of truth, so replaying a run never re-queries reality and gets a different answer.

**Design notes.** `sensor s : T;` read-only, level-triggered (§14); reads recorded to the input tape at commit (input source-of-truth); the wiring that makes sensors live rather than parse-only as in v6.

Source: MP§14. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `sensors-time`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.1.json`.

title: first-class tape values (W7.10)
body:
Tapes become first-class values you can hold, pass, serialize, and inspect — not just an internal recording mechanism but data a machine or its host can manipulate. This is what lets you save a run's tape, ship it in a bug report, diff two runs, or feed a recorded input tape back in to re-drive a machine, turning replay from a built-in feature into a composable capability you build tooling on.

**Design notes.** first-class tape values; construct/serialize/inspect; feed recorded input tapes back to re-drive a machine; the value-level counterpart to the tape wiring (W7.9).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.9.

Source: MP§14. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `tapes`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.10.json`.

title: fsl/fslf/fss/fssf tags + from() family (W7.12)
body:
The file/format tags — fsl, fslf, fss, fssf — distinguishing a bare machine from a factory, a system, and a system-factory, plus a `from()` family for constructing from each. This is the type-tagging that lets tooling and the loader know what shape of thing a document holds, and `from()` is the one uniform entry point for instantiating any of them rather than four bespoke constructors.

**Design notes.** fsl/fslf/fss/fssf tags (machine/factory/system/system-factory); `from()` construction family; `sm` deprecated synonym; the document-kind taxonomy.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.12.json`.

title: System type, delegated API, Steppable/Serializable/Renderable (W7.13)
body:
The System type — a container of wired machines that presents a delegated API and the shared Steppable/Serializable/Renderable interfaces, so a whole system steps, snapshots, and renders through one uniform surface. This is what lets a five-hundred-machine system be driven exactly like a single machine, and it is the interface the web components auto-detect to render either one without special-casing.

**Design notes.** System type + delegated API; Steppable/Serializable/Renderable shared interfaces (wc auto-detects); the uniform surface over a wired system (§22).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.11.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.13.json`.

title: shared-clock stepping + .advance (W7.14)
body:
A system steps on a shared clock — one `.advance` moves every member forward together — so time is coherent across the whole system and a synthetic clock drives all of it deterministically. This is what makes multi-machine time make sense: members don't each carry their own drifting clock, they share one you control, which is exactly what makes whole-system replay reproducible.

**Design notes.** shared-clock stepping + `.advance` (§22); one clock drives all members; the deterministic time base for system replay; the alternative to per-member dispatch.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.14.json`.

title: queued dispatch + depth bound (W7.15)
body:
Deterministic queued dispatch with a depth bound — events between machines are delivered through a FIFO queue in a defined order (document-order routes, write-order emissions, creation-order broadcast), bounded so a dispatch storm can't run away. The determinism is the whole point: same seed and stimuli, bit-identical run, even across hundreds of members, because delivery order is fixed rather than a race.

**Design notes.** deterministic FIFO queued dispatch (doc-order routes, write-order emissions, creation-order broadcast) + queue-depth bound (§22); the determinism guarantee for systems.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.15.json`.

title: routes: facts/targets/where-selectors/sender (W7.16)
body:
Routes — `on fact -> target` — the declarative wiring that says which machine's outputs feed which machine's inputs, with `where` member-selectors to target subsets, observation sugar (enters/exits/takes), and `sender` as context. Routes are how a system is wired without imperative glue: you declare the information flow and the dispatcher makes it happen deterministically, so the wiring reads as a spec rather than a pile of event handlers.

**Design notes.** routes `on fact -> target delivery`; observation sugar enters/exits/takes; `where` member selectors; `sender` context only; the declarative wiring layer.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.16.json`.

title: populations many/count/max (W7.17)
body:
Populations — `many X via factory count E max N` — declare a dynamic group of machines built from a factory, with a required maximum so the system stays finite and checkable. This is how you model "a fleet of workers" or "one session per user" without hand-instantiating each: declare the population, and the required max is what keeps it inside the verifiable band instead of exploding to infinity.

**Design notes.** `many X via factory count E max N` (§22); max required for finiteness; built from a factory (W7.24); members are spawn/retire managed (W7.18).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.17.json`.

title: spawn/retire (W7.18)
body:
Dynamic spawn and retire — machines can be created and removed from a population at runtime, with indices never reused so identity stays stable and a retired member's slot never silently becomes someone else. This is the lifecycle management for dynamic systems: fleets grow and shrink, and the no-reuse rule is what keeps routes and references from quietly pointing at the wrong machine after churn.

**Design notes.** spawn/retire within a population (§22); indices never reused (stable identity); interacts with routes (W7.16) and supervision (W7.20).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.17.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.18.json`.

title: on_undeliverable + dead-letter tape (W7.19)
body:
A policy for messages that can't be delivered — `on_undeliverable: error | drop | dead_letter` — with a dead-letter tape (a third diagnostic tape) capturing the ones you chose to keep. Undeliverable messages are inevitable in a dynamic system (the target retired, the queue was full); making the policy explicit and the dead letters inspectable is the difference between a debuggable system and one that silently drops messages on the floor.

**Design notes.** `on_undeliverable: error|drop|dead_letter` (§22); dead-letter tape (third diagnostic tape); explicit per-system policy; feeds diagnostics.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.19.json`.

title: supervision + restart intensity (W7.20)
body:
Supervision — `on_member_error: escalate | restart | retire` with restart-intensity windows — so failure becomes policy: a crashed member restarts from its factory coordinate, or the error escalates up the containment tree, bounded so a thrashing member can't restart-loop forever. This is Erlang-style let-it-crash for FSL systems: you declare how failure propagates and recovers instead of hoping nothing ever fails.

**Design notes.** `on_member_error: escalate|restart|retire` + restart-intensity windows (§22); restart from the factory coordinate (W7.25); escalation up the containment tree (W7.22).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.18.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `supervision-hierarchy`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.20.json`.

title: dispatch fairness + quotas (W7.21)
body:
Dispatch fairness and quotas — `dispatch: fifo | fair` plus per-member quotas — so no single machine can starve the others or monopolize the dispatcher. FIFO is deterministic-simple; fair dispatch with quotas is what keeps a busy member from hogging the whole system, which starts to matter once populations get large and their traffic uneven.

**Design notes.** `dispatch: fifo|fair` + per-member quotas (§22); prevents starvation/monopoly; fair mode stays deterministic under the declared policy.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.15.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.21.json`.

title: hierarchy, containment-acyclic enforcement (W7.22)
body:
Systems nest — a system can contain sub-systems — with the containment tree compile-enforced acyclic even though the wiring between machines may cycle. This separation is the key insight: information can flow in loops, but ownership can't, which is what makes supervision escalation and recursive snapshots well-defined. Containment is a tree; wiring is a graph; keeping them distinct is what keeps the system reasoning sound.

**Design notes.** nested systems; containment tree compile-enforced acyclic (wiring may cycle); child dispatch to quiescence, emissions bubble; the ownership structure supervision escalates up.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `supervision-hierarchy`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.22.json`.

title: system serialization (recursive snapshot) (W7.23)
body:
A whole system serializes as a recursive snapshot — every member machine, the dispatch queue, the routes, the populations, captured together so an entire running system can be saved and restored exactly. This is replay and persistence scaled from one machine to a whole system: the snapshot is the system's complete state, and restoring it resumes every member precisely where it left off.

**Design notes.** recursive system snapshot (§22); captures members + queue + populations; nested member snapshots; the system-level replay/persistence unit.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§22. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.23.json`.

title: seed tree derive() pinned + vectors + edit-stability (W7.25)
body:
The seed tree — `derive(u64, label)` deterministically derives a child seed from a parent, so instance 42 of seed 1234 is a fixed coordinate reproducible forever and parallelizable trivially. The derivation is pinned with vectors and an edit-stability policy because it is an irreversible: once machines are built at these coordinates, the derivation can never change without breaking every reproduction that ever relied on it.

**Design notes.** `derive(u64,label)` = FNV-1a64 + splitmix64 round, pinned with vectors + edit-stability policy (irreversibles #11); the reproducible-coordinate tree; instance N of seed S is fixed forever.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.24.

Source: MP§21. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `factories`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.25.json`.

title: at/make/with + UnboundParameterError + Factory serialize (W7.26)
body:
The factory's construction API — pure `at(n)` (the instance at a coordinate), cursor `make()` (the next instance), immutable-curry `with()` (bind some parameters, defer the rest) — plus `UnboundParameterError` that lists every parameter you forgot, and a serialize contract capturing source hash, bindings, seed, and cursor. This is what makes factories ergonomic and resumable: a clear error for missing parameters, and a construction state you can save and continue.

**Design notes.** pure `at(n)` / cursor `make()` / immutable-curry `with()`; UnboundParameterError lists all unbound; serialize {source_hash, bindings-as-text, seed, cursor}.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.24.

Source: MP§21. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `factories`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.26.json`.

title: §24 system viz (W7.28)
body:
System visualization — render a whole system as a diagram with member clusters, ×N population badges, route edges, and a dead-letter sink — so a system is as inspectable at a glance as a single machine is. Visualizing composition is what keeps large systems comprehensible; without it, a five-hundred-machine system is an opaque blob instead of a picture you can actually reason about and debug.

**Design notes.** §24 system viz; member clusters, ×N population badges, route edges, dead-letter sink; the system-scale counterpart to single-machine rendering.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.13.

Source: MP§24. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `systems-dispatch`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.28.json`.

title: emit-in-transaction (W7.3)
body:
Emission is transactional — `emit c <- expr` buffers the output and flushes it only when the macrostep commits, so an output caused by a step that then rolls back never escapes. This is what makes FSL's outputs trustworthy: the world only ever sees emissions from steps that actually happened, never from a transaction that was undone — the difference between exactly-once and an at-least-once mess someone downstream has to de-duplicate.

**Design notes.** `emit c <- expr` buffers in the emission set E, flushes at commit (C2 macrostep); rolled back with the step that caused it; the output dual of the transactional rollback (W6.69).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.2.

Source: MP§14. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `channels-emit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.3.json`.

title: statechart set per decision (W7.30)
body:
The classic statechart features, landed as a coherent set per the umbrella decision: history pseudostates, final states with done-events, fork/join (parallel regions), in-state guards, internal transitions, deferred events, raise/send, and activities (do/invoke). This is FSL reaching feature-parity with UML and SCXML statecharts, done deliberately as one designed set rather than piecemeal, so the pieces compose cleanly instead of colliding when combined.

**Design notes.** history #1340, final #1341, fork/join #1342, in-state #1343, internal #1344, deferred #1347, raise/send #1346, activities #1351; the decided statechart set under umbrella #1353 (parallel regions stays open by decision).

Source: fsl#1340, fsl#1341, fsl#1342, fsl#1343, fsl#1344, fsl#1346, fsl#1347, fsl#1351, fsl#1353. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `status-recovery-rest`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.30.json`.

title: error-recovery transitions (W7.31)
body:
Error-recovery transitions — `require X else -> S` and `on error where kind=K -> S` — so a failed guard or a specific error routes to a recovery state instead of just faulting. This is what lets a machine handle its own failures gracefully, turning "contract violated" from a dead end into a modeled transition to a state that knows how to recover, retry, or degrade.

**Design notes.** `require X else -> S` / `on error where kind=K -> S` (§11); routes violations/errors to recovery states; uses Error.kind (W6.66).

Source: MP§11. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `status-recovery-rest`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.31.json`.

title: units §4.5 + SI prelude (W7.32)
body:
Physical units — phantom dimensions with an SI prelude — so a val can be typed as meters or seconds and the checker catches adding a length to a time, with zero runtime cost because units are erased after checking. This is the Mars-Climate-Orbiter class of bug (mixing unit systems) made a compile error, and it costs nothing at runtime because it is purely a type-level check that vanishes in the compiled machine.

**Design notes.** §4.5 units (phantom dimensions + SI prelude), zero runtime; dimension mismatch is a type error; erased after checking.

Source: MP§4.5. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `status-recovery-rest`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.32.json`.

title: windowed aggregates (W7.33)
body:
Windowed aggregates — `rate/count/avg(expr, N)` — that summarize the last N values of an expression, lowering to fixed N-slot ring buffers so they stay finite and checkable. This is the common streaming-analytics need (a moving average, a recent rate) made a language primitive over a bounded window, so a machine can react to trends without the aggregate growing unbounded and blowing the finite band.

**Design notes.** `rate/count/avg(expr,N)` lower to N-slot rings (finite-eligible); windowed over the last N; builds on the stats family (W6.51).

Source: MP§7. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `status-recovery-rest`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.33.json`.

title: repl + make verbs (W7.36)
body:
The `repl` and `make` verbs — an interactive read-eval-print loop for driving a machine step by step at the command line, and `make` for constructing instances from factories. These are the hands-on tools for exploring and operating machines: `repl` is how you poke at a machine live to understand it, `make` is how you instantiate one from a factory without writing any host code.

**Design notes.** `repl` interactive loop + `make` factory-construction verb (§25); jssm twins #630/#8; the interactive-operation surface.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `status-recovery-rest`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.36.json`.

title: docs-extraction artifact (W7.37)
body:
The docs-extraction artifact — the fifth build artifact that pulls the doc-comments written since v6 out of a machine and emits reference documentation. This is where FSL's doc-comments finally pay off: a machine's states, transitions, and vals become browsable reference docs generated from the source, the way TypeDoc generates documentation from TypeScript rather than making you write it twice.

**Design notes.** §16 docs-extraction artifact (the fifth artifact); consumes doc-comments (W6.111); emits reference docs; the manual program's generated-docs input.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.111.

Source: MP§16. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `status-recovery-rest`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.37.json`.

title: on chan(payload) heads (W7.4)
body:
Transition heads that bind a typed payload — `on c(payload) ->` — so a transition can name and use the data an event carries, not just react to its name. This is what makes typed channels actually useful: the payload flows into the transition's guards and assignments as a bound, typed value, so "on deposit(amount)" can reference the amount instead of the event having to stash its data somewhere on the side.

**Design notes.** `on c(payload) ->` heads bind typed channel payloads (fsl#1348); the payload is available to guards/assigns; per-channel type-checked.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.2.

Source: MP§14, fsl#1348. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `channels-emit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.4.json`.

title: Mealy + Moore emit points (W7.5)
body:
Both classic emit disciplines: Mealy outputs (emitted on a transition edge, depending on the input) and Moore outputs (emitted on state entry, depending only on the state). Supporting both means FSL can model the full range of finite-state-machine output styles from the textbooks directly, instead of forcing every design into one convention and making the other awkward to express.

**Design notes.** Mealy (edge) + Moore (entry) emit points (§14); both lower to the transactional emit (W7.3); the output-timing choice is per-emission.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W7.3.

Source: MP§14. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `channels-emit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.5.json`.

title: named hooks: declaration/required/call-position (W7.6)
body:
Named hooks as a machine's dependency contract — callbacks the machine declares (`hook f(a:T)->R [required]`), that the caller must supply, typed like everything else and called at explicit positions in the logic. This is how a machine states "I need you to provide this behavior," turning host integration from ad-hoc wiring into a checked interface, and return values are recorded so replay never re-fires the outside world.

**Design notes.** `hook f(a:T)->R [required];` declaration; call-position `call f(x)` / `assign y := call f(x)`; effect boundary per §11; the return-recording format lands v13 (slot reserved) — v11 ships call semantics + live mode.

Source: MP§12, MP§14. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `named-hooks`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.6.json`.

title: tokenized_by composition (W7.8)
body:
`tokenized_by` — compose one machine's output as another's tokenized input, so a lexer-machine can feed a parser-machine, or any producer can drive any consumer through a typed token stream. This is a composition primitive: it lets you build a pipeline of machines where each stage's output alphabet becomes the next stage's input, with the handoff checked for compatibility instead of hoped-for.

**Design notes.** `tokenized_by` composition (§14); output-to-tokenized-input wiring; alphabet compatibility checked; a building block for multi-machine systems.

Source: MP§14. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `tapes`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W7.8.json`.

title: tests-are-tapes promotion flow (W8.10)
body:
A flow that promotes a test into a tape and a tape into a test — a shrunk fuzz failure becomes a permanent regression tape, and a recorded run becomes an assertion. This is the continuum that unifies testing, fuzzing, and replay: every interesting run can become a durable test, so bugs found by fuzzing never come back, and the test suite grows itself from real executions instead of only from what someone thought to write.

**Design notes.** tests-are-tapes promotion (both directions); a shrunk fuzz failure becomes a permanent tape; recorded runs become assertions; the fuzz->regression pipeline.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.9.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `testing-toolkit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.10.json`.

title: FSM-shaped coverage incl. guard MC-DC (W8.12)
body:
Coverage metrics shaped for state machines — state, transition, path, and guard MC-DC (modified condition/decision coverage) — plus standard vacuity checks, so "is this machine well-tested" has an answer tuned to the way machines actually fail. Guard MC-DC in particular catches the boolean-condition gaps that line coverage misses entirely, which is exactly where guard bugs like to hide from an ordinary test suite.

**Design notes.** state/transition/path coverage + guard MC-DC; vacuity checks standard; FSM-shaped rather than line-shaped; measures machine test adequacy.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `testing-toolkit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.12.json`.

title: F6 composed checking (W8.13)
body:
Verify a whole system without the state-space product blowing up — each member proves its interface contract once, and the system composes those contracts instead of exploring every combined state. This is what makes the five-hundred-machine park verifiable: it becomes one proof per member plus a composition argument, rather than an intractable product of five hundred state spaces that no checker could ever finish.

**Design notes.** finite-system = members finite + population max + queue bound (compiler-enforced); checks bound safety, lifted contracts, deliverability, invariant over count/all/any/sum, opt-in deadlock; the compositional scaling story.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.2.

Source: MP§23. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `composed-checking`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.13.json`.

title: assume-guarantee composition (W8.14)
body:
Assume-guarantee reasoning — each member declares what it assumes of its environment and guarantees in return, and the system is proven correct by discharging those obligations against each other, with session-typed wiring checks from the interfaces. This is the formal machinery behind composed checking: it is how you turn "each part is correct" into "the whole is correct" without re-proving the whole system from scratch every time.

**Design notes.** member interface contracts (assumes/guarantees) + composition argument; session-typed wiring checks from interfaces; the A/G machinery under F6 (W8.13).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.13.

Source: MP§23. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `composed-checking`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.14.json`.

title: flow contracts (disallow flow a->b) (W8.15)
body:
Flow contracts — `disallow flow a -> b` — that forbid information from flowing between designated parts of a system, checked as taint-product reachability. This is the security and privacy primitive: assert that secret data can never reach a public output and have the checker prove it, which is exactly the kind of property that is easy to state, catastrophic to get wrong, and impossible to test exhaustively.

**Design notes.** `disallow flow a->b` = taint-product reachability; information-flow contracts; proves non-interference; a security/privacy checking primitive.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.13.

Source: MP§23. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `composed-checking`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.15.json`.

title: SLO contracts (design-time half) (W8.16)
body:
Service-level-objective contracts expressed as PCTL in contract position — "99.9% of requests settle within 100 steps" as a design-time obligation the checker verifies against the model. This is the design-time half of SLOs (the runtime-monitor half comes in the survival era): it lets you state performance and reliability targets as verifiable properties of the machine, not just hopes you measure after deployment and discover you missed.

**Design notes.** SLO contracts = PCTL in contract position (design-time half); verified against the model; the runtime-monitor half is era 4; builds on the probabilistic tier (W8.5).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.5.

Source: MP§23. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `composed-checking`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.16.json`.

title: diff --behavioral + refinement certificates + free-theorem tape reuse (W8.17)
body:
Upgrades get proofs — `diff --behavioral` shows that v2 simulates v1 (every old behavior is preserved), refinement certificates make that proof portable, and old test tapes replay on v2 for free via the simulation map. This is what makes evolving a machine safe: you prove the new version is a faithful refinement of the old, and your entire existing test corpus carries forward without a single test rewrite.

**Design notes.** simulation preorder over the old alphabet (decidable finite); refinement certs {v1_hash, v2_hash, verdict}; free theorem: v1 tapes replay on v2 by the simulation map; the safe-evolution toolchain.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `evolution-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.17.json`.

title: minimize (bisimulation quotient) (W8.18)
body:
`minimize` — reduce a machine to its smallest behaviorally-equivalent form via a Paige-Tarjan bisimulation quotient, merging states that are indistinguishable. This is classic automata minimization made a verb: a cleaner, smaller machine that provably behaves identically, useful for comprehension, for faster checking, and for canonicalizing a machine before you compare it against another.

**Design notes.** `minimize` = Paige-Tarjan bisimulation quotient; the smallest behaviorally-equivalent machine; provably behavior-preserving; aids checking and canonicalization.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `evolution-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.18.json`.

title: M2 signing + --emit-certificate signed (W8.19)
body:
Milestone 2 of the trust stack — keyless signing lands at the latest here, so `--emit-certificate` produces a signed certificate rather than the bare record from v9. This is where a verification result becomes something you can hand to a third party as evidence: not just "I checked it" but a cryptographically signed claim bound to the exact source, verifiable by anyone who doesn't trust you and shouldn't have to.

**Design notes.** M2 keyless signing; `--emit-certificate` signed (upgrades the unsigned M1 record, W6.96); the signature layer over the certificate; may have attempted earlier in era 1, lands here at the latest; jssm twin #826.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.96.

Source: MP§17, notes/superpowers/plans/2026-06-26-signed-run-receipts-roadmap.md. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `evolution-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.19.json`.

title: check --witness/--estimate full (W8.20)
body:
The full `check` verb surface — `--witness` to get the coverage facts and evidence behind a verdict, and a complete `--estimate` of verification cost. This matures the honesty layer from v9's basic tri-state into a checker that shows its work: not just "safe" but "safe, and here is the witness," and not just "this might be expensive" but a real cost estimate before you commit the compute.

**Design notes.** `check --witness` (coverage facts / evidence) + full `--estimate`; matures the v9 budget/undecided tri-state (W6.98); the checker shows its work.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W6.98.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `evolution-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.20.json`.

title: lsp live-verification daemon (W8.21)
body:
A language-server check-daemon that verifies as you type — incremental, hash-keyed, budgeted — so verification squiggles appear in the editor like type errors, not as a separate batch step you remember to run later. This is what makes verification part of the writing loop instead of a gate: you see a property fail the moment you break it, while the reasoning is still fresh in your head.

**Design notes.** lsp check-daemon (incremental, hash-keyed, budgeted squiggles); live verification in the editor; makes checking part of the edit loop.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `evolution-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.21.json`.

title: mcp debugging verb — time-travel run interrogation (W8.22)
body:
The debugging half of the `mcp` verb: time-travel tools — state_at, why_rejected, val_history, diverges_from — that let an agent interrogate a recorded run at any point. This is the payoff of deterministic replay: because every run is reproducible, you can step backward, ask why a transition was rejected, or find exactly where two runs diverged — impossible with a live-only debugger. Split from the authoring MCP (Wmcp.1), which ships years earlier; this half waits for the v9-v12 runtime + proof stack it interrogates.

**Design notes.** Time-travel tools (state_at / why_rejected / val_history / diverges_from) over recorded runs; the replay-powered debugger, layered onto the fsl-mcp satellite once run-records + proofs exist.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Wmcp.1.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.22.json`.

title: certified stdlib first parts (W8.23)
body:
The first certified standard-library components — debounce, retry, breaker, limiter, saga, turnstile — each shipping with an interface card stating its verified properties. These are the reusable, proven building blocks the whole verification stack was built to enable: instead of everyone reimplementing a circuit breaker slightly wrong, you compose a certified one whose guarantees are already proven and travel with it in its certificate.

**Design notes.** certified stdlib first parts (debounce/retry/breaker/limiter/saga/turnstile) with interface cards; reusable proven components; needs the certificate machinery (W8.19).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.19.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `evolution-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.23.json`.

title: backend lowerings --via nuxmv/spin/storm + agreement tests (W8.3)
body:
FSL doesn't build a world-class model checker — it compiles to three of them (nuXmv, Spin, Storm) and requires them to agree, so a verdict is trustworthy because independent tools confirmed it. The differential agreement harness is the trick: if the backends disagree, that is a bug in a lowering, caught before a wrong verdict ever ships. Compile-to-the-experts rather than reinvent-the-expert, and cross-check the experts against each other.

**Design notes.** `check --via nuxmv|spin|storm` lowerings (SMV/Promela/PRISM) from the IR; differential verdict-agreement harness; budgets/undecided first-class across all backends.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `backends-bridges`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.3.json`.

title: export --target tla+/alloy/smv/promela (W8.4)
body:
Export a machine to the formal-methods languages researchers already use — TLA+, Alloy, SMV, Promela — so FSL becomes a bridge into the existing verification ecosystem rather than an island. The same lowerings that power the checker double as export targets, which means a formal-methods researcher can study an FSL machine in their own tools, and FSL benefits from their scrutiny in return.

**Design notes.** `export --target tla+|alloy|smv|promela`; reuses the backend lowerings (W8.3); the bridge for formal-methods researchers; a machine becomes studiable in established tools.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.3.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `backends-bridges`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.4.json`.

title: PCTL / probabilistic tier (W8.5)
body:
A probabilistic verification tier — PCTL properties like "the probability of failure is at most 0.001" and expected-cost queries — via the Storm backend. This is what lets FSL verify the stochastic machines it can already model: a machine with weighted transitions or random draws gets probabilistic guarantees, not just yes/no safety, which is what reliability and performance modeling actually need.

**Design notes.** PCTL via Storm (`P>=x [...]`, expected-cost); the probabilistic verification tier; verifies stochastic machines (weighted edges W7.34, rand W6.52); budgets/undecided apply.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.3.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `backends-bridges`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.5.json`.

title: past operators (W8.6)
body:
Past-time temporal operators — once, since — for talking about what has already happened in a run, which is exactly what you need to monitor a tape after the fact ("did we ever see X before Y"). Past operators are the natural fit for runtime monitoring and post-hoc analysis, complementing the future-time operators used for ahead-of-time verification.

**Design notes.** past operators (once/since) for tape monitoring; complement the future-time temporal language (W8.2); the natural form for runtime property checking over recorded tapes.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.2.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `temporal-language`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.6.json`.

title: settles_within(k) (W8.7)
body:
A `settles_within(k)` property — assert that the machine reaches a stable configuration within k steps — via bounded exploration. This is the practical liveness question most people actually have ("does this converge, and how fast"), answered by bounded model checking so it stays decidable, and it is the bridge from pure safety properties toward real-time-ish guarantees about responsiveness.

**Design notes.** `settles_within(k)` via bounded exploration; bounded liveness; decidable by construction; the convergence question over an explicit step budget.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W8.2.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `temporal-language`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.7.json`.

title: in-language test/expect blocks + assertion vocabulary (W8.9)
body:
Tests written in FSL itself — `test`/`expect` blocks with a rich assertion vocabulary (reaches, blocked, invariant-holds, temporal, IO-relation, replay-equivalence, coverage) — so a machine's tests live with the machine and codegen-export alongside it. This collapses the gap between the machine and its tests: an assertion can itself be a model-check, and the tests travel wherever the machine is compiled instead of being stranded in a separate host harness.

**Design notes.** in-language `test`/`expect` with assertions (reaches/blocked/invariant-holds/temporal/IO-relation/replay-equivalence/coverage); codegen-exported; tests live with the machine.

Source: MP§17. Part of #1407 (v12 umbrella). cluster `testing-toolkit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W8.9.json`.

title: inspect --redact + redaction map proof (W9.10)
body:
Provably-safe redaction — `inspect --redact` uses a verifier-emitted map to pseudonymize data that no guard reads, so a redacted tape stays bit-identical on replay while sensitive values are removed. This is what lets you share a run for debugging or compliance without leaking secrets: the verifier proves which data is load-bearing (and flags it) versus safely removable, so redaction can never accidentally change the machine's behavior.

**Design notes.** `inspect --redact` via a verifier-emitted map (data unread-by-guards -> pseudonymize, replay stays bit-identical; load-bearing data flagged); provably-safe redaction.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `forensics`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.10.json`.

title: bug verb + CI auto-replay (W9.11)
body:
`bug` produces a self-proving bug report — a redacted repro bundle plus a narrated trace, with CI auto-replay labels so the report re-runs itself in the pipeline. This is the endgame of replay plus redaction: a bug report that carries its own reproduction, redacted safely, and re-executes automatically, so "cannot reproduce" stops being a valid response and a fixed bug can be proven fixed.

**Design notes.** `bug` = redacted bundle (W9.10) + narrated trace + CI auto-replay labels; the self-reproducing bug report.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W9.10.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `forensics`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.11.json`.

title: doctor verb (W9.12)
body:
`doctor` — an environment diagnostic that reports the host, runtime versions, store configuration, and anything else that affects whether a machine will run and replay correctly. The mundane but essential "why doesn't this work on my machine" tool: it surfaces the environment table so a support conversation starts from facts instead of twenty rounds of questions about what version of what is installed where.

**Design notes.** `doctor` env table; host/runtime/store/config diagnostics; the environment-facts tool for support.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `forensics`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.12.json`.

title: 5.x snapshot-lift decision (W9.13)
body:
A recorded decision — do 5.x-era serializations lift into v6-and-later, or get refused? — written down even if the answer is "refuse," because leaving it implicit is exactly how a silent corruption bug ships. This is one of two policy decisions this era owes: whether the oldest snapshots are worth carrying forward, or whether the clean break is safer, decided deliberately and on the record rather than by whatever the code happens to do.

**Design notes.** D9 decision: 5.x serializations lift-or-refuse, written down (omissions D9); either answer is acceptable, silence is not; interacts with version-gating (W6.93).

Source: MP§15. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `policy-decisions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.13.json`.

title: §29 security/effects appendix (W9.14)
body:
The security and effects appendix — the normative document describing FSL's effect boundary, persistence surface, and their security implications, drafted here with input from the trust era's security review. This is the written contract for what a machine can and cannot do to the outside world, which the fleet era's host implementations must honor and the trust era's threat model is built on top of.

**Design notes.** §29 security/effects-model appendix, drafted with era-5/v14 security-review input; the normative effect-boundary + persistence-surface contract; feeds the fleet ABI (W11.1).

Source: MP§29. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `policy-decisions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.14.json`.

title: recorded hook returns + determinism ledger (W9.2)
body:
The effect boundary made real — external calls record their answers, and replay consumes the recordings instead of re-firing the world. This is what makes a machine with side effects still replayable, and it is what finally lets receipts cover hooked machines: the v14 scope limit that said "only pure machines can be attested" dies right here. An irreversible, because the recording format is pinned forever.

**Design notes.** fills irreversibles #4; per-call record {call-site id, args-hash, return, seq} in a sidecar ledger beside the tape (tape stays stimulus-pure); replay: missing recording = refusal (§15), re-fire only under explicit `--live`; idempotency annotations honored after crash.

Source: MP§15, MP§11. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `effect-recording`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.2.json`.

title: migrates-from + verifier-checked lifting (W9.3)
body:
Deploy v2 while v1 instances are still mid-flight — a declared, verifier-checked lifting of old snapshots into the new val schema, so an in-progress machine can be upgraded without losing its state. `migrates from v1 { new := expr(old...) }` is the migration, and the checker proves the lifted state is in-domain and the contracts still hold, so you can't ship a migration that quietly corrupts every live machine it touches.

**Design notes.** `migrates from v1 { new := expr(old...); }` blocks; checker verifies lifted states in-domain + contracts hold + refinement verdict; the safe schema-evolution mechanism for live machines.

Source: MP§15. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `migration-upgrade`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.3.json`.

title: hot member upgrade (W9.4)
body:
Live member replacement in a running system — retire a member with a snapshot, lift it through `migrates from`, and restart it from the v2 factory inside dispatch, without stopping the system. With supervision and the migration machinery already in place, hot upgrade simply falls out: a fleet can be upgraded member by member while it keeps serving, no maintenance window required.

**Design notes.** hot upgrade = retire-with-snapshot -> lift (W9.3) -> restart from v2 factory inside dispatch; live member replacement; builds on supervision (W7.20) + migration (W9.3).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W9.3.

Source: MP§15, MP§22. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `migration-upgrade`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.4.json`.

title: canary factories (W9.5)
body:
Weighted canary cohorts — `via factory weighted [v1 90% v2 10%]` — so a new version rolls out to a small fraction of a population first, with cohort drift comparison to catch a regression before it is everywhere. This is progressive delivery for machine fleets, falling out of the factory + population + migration machinery: canary the risky version, compare the cohorts on live behavior, then promote or roll back.

**Design notes.** `via factory weighted [v1 90% v2 10%]` + cohort drift comparison; progressive rollout over populations (W7.17); builds on migration (W9.3).

**Dependencies.** Depends on W9.3.

Source: MP§15, MP§22. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `migration-upgrade`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.5.json`.

title: OTel flight-recorder mapping + one-call bundle export (W9.6)
body:
The tape is already the telemetry — an OpenTelemetry mapping makes machines native to any observability stack (a transition is a span, val deltas are attributes, a machine or system is a resource), plus a one-call export of a repro-bundle per trace window. This means a machine's production behavior shows up in Datadog or Honeycomb like any other service, and any interesting window can be exported as a reproducible bundle for offline debugging.

**Design notes.** OTel mapping (transition=span/event, val deltas=attrs, machine/system=resource) + one-call repro-bundle export per trace window; the flight recorder; makes machines observability-native.

Source: MP§15. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `flight-recorder`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.6.json`.

title: behavioral drift detection (W9.7)
body:
Turn statistical surprise into pre-outage incidents — compare live transition statistics against the machine's own Markov prediction, and alert when reality diverges from the model beyond a threshold. Drift detection is early warning: when a machine starts behaving differently than it provably should, that is a signal something upstream changed, caught before it becomes an outage rather than diagnosed in the postmortem after.

**Design notes.** drift = live transition stats vs §17 Markov prediction (χ² or KL threshold per edge); pre-outage early warning; feeds calibrate (W9.8).

Source: MP§15, MP§17. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `flight-recorder`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.7.json`.

title: calibrate verb (W9.8)
body:
`calibrate` closes the loop — correct the model's weights and bindings from production data, emitted as a reviewable diff you approve, never a silent change. When drift detection shows the model has gone stale, calibrate proposes the correction; because it arrives as a diff, a human sees exactly what production taught the model before it is re-proven against the updated numbers.

**Design notes.** `calibrate` emits weight/binding corrections as a reviewable diff, never silent; closes the drift loop (W9.7); the corrected model is re-proven.

**Dependencies.** Depends on W9.7.

Source: MP§15. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `flight-recorder`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.8.json`.

title: run --amend counterfactual (W9.9)
body:
Counterfactual replay — `run --amend val=v` or `--amend-input step=N` forks a recorded run with one thing changed, and hands both to `diff` for a first-divergence report. This is "what if" debugging: change one input or value in a real run and see exactly where and how the outcome diverges, which is how you test a hypothesis about a bug without having to reproduce the whole scenario live and hope it happens again.

**Design notes.** `run --amend val=v` / `--amend-input step=N` forks a recorded run; handed to `diff` for a first-divergence report; counterfactual debugging over replay.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `forensics`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/W9.9.json`.

title: fsl-mcp authoring satellite — validate/render/explain/simulate/lint (Wmcp.1)
body:
An MCP server that lets an AI agent make FSL machines: the agent writes the FSL, and the server validates it, renders the diagram, explains the states and transitions, simulates a walk, and lints for quality. Every tool wraps capability the library already ships (parse, validate, visualize, step), so this is a thin satellite package that can land immediately — years ahead of the full v12 debugging verb — and is the fastest path to authoring FSL with an agent. STANDING RULE: each era that adds authorable language surface ships a matching fsl-mcp update (the Wmcp.* sync items), so the tool never lags the language.

**Design notes.** Standalone npm package `fsl-mcp`, a stdio MCP server over the current jssm API. Tools: fsl_validate(source)->diagnostics; fsl_render(source)->svg; fsl_explain(source)->state/transition summary; fsl_simulate(source, actions)->state + legal next actions; fsl_lint(source)->quality notes. No runtime or proof dependencies; grows via the per-era Wmcp.* sync items. Spec: notes/superpowers/specs/2026-07-07-fsl-mcp-authoring-satellite.md.

**Dependencies.** blocks W8.22, Wmcp.2.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1401 (v6 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/Wmcp.1.json`.

title: fsl-mcp sync: expose the v7 expression surface (Wmcp.2)
body:
Keep fsl-mcp current with v7. When the expression language, numeric tower, strings, and where-guards land, the authoring tools must validate, explain, and where relevant evaluate them — so agents author v7 machines with full feedback instead of the tool silently lagging the grammar.

**Design notes.** Extend fsl_validate / fsl_explain / fsl_simulate to cover expressions, sized numerics, and where-guards; surface expression diagnostics. Tracks the P2 slices.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Wmcp.1.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1402 (v7 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/Wmcp.2.json`.

title: fsl-mcp sync: expose the v8 container/ADT/graph surface (Wmcp.3)
body:
Keep fsl-mcp current with v8. Containers, ADTs, streams, function-slots, overlapping groups, and the graph form become authorable, so the MCP's validate/explain/render must understand and describe them for the agent.

**Design notes.** Extend fsl_explain / fsl_render for groups and graph:{}; validate container/ADT constructs; surface exhaustiveness diagnostics.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Wmcp.2.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1403 (v8 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/Wmcp.3.json`.

title: fsl-mcp sync: expose the v9 contract/assign/hook surface (Wmcp.4)
body:
Keep fsl-mcp current with v9. assign, the contract quartet (require/ensure/invariant), RTC, and hooks become authorable, so the MCP must validate contracts and explain the transactional and hook semantics it can now see.

**Design notes.** Validate require/ensure/invariant; explain RTC and hook timing; simulate with assign/journal semantics where feasible before the proof stack exists.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Wmcp.3.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1404 (v9 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/Wmcp.4.json`.

title: fsl-mcp sync: expose the v10 format/CST + conformance surface (Wmcp.5)
body:
Keep fsl-mcp current with v10. Byte-level reversibility (CST + format) and the conformance/portability contract land, so the MCP should expose an fsl_format tool and report conformance so agents author portable, canonically-formatted machines.

**Design notes.** Add fsl_format(source)->formatted via the v10 format verb; surface conformance-vector results; keep tool output canonical.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Wmcp.4.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/Wmcp.5.json`.

title: fsl-mcp sync: expose the v11 systems/factories/statechart surface (Wmcp.6)
body:
Keep fsl-mcp current with v11. Systems, factories, supervision, and statechart structure (hierarchy, parallel regions) become authorable, so the MCP must validate and explain composed multi-machine structure, not just single machines.

**Design notes.** Extend fsl_validate / fsl_explain / fsl_render for systems, factories, and statechart hierarchy/parallel regions; describe supervision topology.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Wmcp.5.

Source: MP§25. Part of #1406 (v11 umbrella). cluster `mcp-tooling`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/Wmcp.6.json`.

title: C3 grammar appendix (WP10.1)
body:
The normative grammar appendix — one authoritative, compact grammar for all the new v6-and-later constructs (declarations, expressions, assign, where, contracts, weighted label lists, channels, factories, system headers) — the document a second implementation parses against and the source the grammar exports are generated from. The proof-construct slice (fsl#818) ships first because it unblocks the example corpus. Without a pinned grammar, "valid FSL" is just whatever the one parser happens to accept that day.

**Design notes.** C3 normative grammar appendix (era-1 WP-1.3, §29); fsl#818 proof-construct slice first (unblocks corpus #817); rides the generator/parser dual; the source for grammar exports (W6.108) and format (W6.102).

Source: era1/WP-1.3, MP§29, fsl#818. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `portability-contract`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP10.1.json`.

title: C4 conformance-vector format + seed corpus (WP10.2)
body:
The conformance-vector format — a document plus a seed plus stimuli, producing a canonical trace and digest — and the first corpus of vectors, seeded by promoting M3's determinism tests. This is the executable definition of "correct FSL behavior": every normative feature ships a vector, and any implementation that produces a different trace fails. It is the artifact that makes "portable" checkable rather than aspirational, and it cannot precede C2 because the trace events are named by the operational semantics.

**Design notes.** schema `fsl.vector.v1` (document, seed, stimuli -> canonical trace + digest); seeded by promoting M3 determinism tests; standing rule: no normative feature without a vector; thin-harness contract; depends on C2 (WP6.6) for trace-event names and C1 (W6.99) for the digest.

**Dependencies.** Depends on WP6.6, W6.99.

Source: era1/WP-1.4, MP§26. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `portability-contract`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP10.2.json`.

title: C5 second implementation + differential CI (WP10.3)
body:
The second implementation — a Rust runtime that consumes the compiled IR rather than reparsing FSL — plus differential CI that runs both implementations over the full vector corpus and byte-compares canonical traces, failing on any disagreement. This is what makes N=2 real: portability stops being a promise and becomes a gate, because the instant two implementations diverge, CI goes red. It also triggers the IR freeze, and the WASM toolchain falls out of the Rust build for free.

**Design notes.** Rust T1 runtime consuming the compiled IR/JSON (no second parser); differential CI byte-compares canonical traces over the vector corpus; nightly full + PR smoke slice; triggers the IR freeze (irreversibles #14); depends on C4 (WP10.2).

**Dependencies.** Depends on WP10.2.

Source: era1/WP-1.5, MP§26. Part of #1405 (v10 umbrella). cluster `portability-contract`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP10.3.json`.

title: the durable-execution PROCESS run (design gate) (WP13.1)
body:
Before any survival code is written, the two-model blind-design cycle — the same PROCESS the receipts spec went through — because this era was priced at a paragraph in the corpus but is actually Temporal's hardest terrain: durable execution done right. The output of that design cycle IS this era's real spec. The paragraph is a placeholder for work that has to be designed carefully and adversarially, or it will be subtly, expensively wrong.

**Design notes.** PROCESS.md 00-09 flow; the cold brief covers persistence contract, effect recording, crash-mid-macrostep, delivery semantics, snapshot schema evolution; conformance-sketch gate before any normative text.

Source: notes/superpowers/specs/blind-thinkthrough/PROCESS.md. Part of #1408 (v13 umbrella). cluster `process-gate`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP13.1.json`.

title: security-adversarial review (design gate) (WP14.1)
body:
Before M2's spec hardens, a security-lensed pass attacks the trust stack's known soft spots — it has had logic review but never a hostile one, and a trust system nobody has tried to break is a trust system that will break in production against someone who did try. The findings amend the receipts design. This is the external-specialist gate the blind-thinkthrough process requires for security-critical work.

**Design notes.** agenda on record: transparency-log split-view/equivocation (witness cosigning or gossip; offline inclusion proofs alone can't detect forks), OIDC-provider compromise as keyless root, single-receipt revocation, presentation replay; satisfies the PROCESS external-specialist gate.

Source: notes/superpowers/plans/2026-06-26-signed-run-receipts-roadmap.md, MP§29. Part of #1409 (v14 umbrella). cluster `security-gate`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP14.1.json`.

title: June implementation-audit re-verification (WP5x.1)
body:
June's implementation audit found packaging and fidelity problems — pack shape, the interchange envelope, forced-transition erasure in codegen — but against a tree that has since moved, so its findings may now be memories rather than facts. This is the pass that reproduces or refutes each of the seven findings against both current main and the assembly line, fixes the cheap ones as 5.x patches, and feeds the rest into the bug burn-down. It is what lets 6.0 fix real problems instead of stale ones.

**Design notes.** reproduce/refute the seven findings on both branches (pack-shape via `npm pack --dry-run --json` assertions; advertised-verb/bin drift; interchange envelope + start-states loss; forced-transition erasure; config-schema drift; test type-import drift); append a dated verdict table to the era-0 brief Corrections; land the pack-shape test green.

Source: era0/WP-1, megaspec-critique.md. Part of #1418 (5.x umbrella). cluster `june-audit`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP5x.1.json`.

title: bare-functions API split (§27) (WP6.1)
body:
For years consumers asked for tree-shaking and couldn't have it: the 420-method Machine class means every bundle carries the whole library whether you call it or not. 6.0 makes `import { create, transition } from 'jssm'` the default — free functions over a plain state record, so a bundler can drop what you don't touch — while the entire 5.x class survives intact at `jssm/compat`. Legacy users change one import line, and that the contract still holds is proven by running the whole 5.x suite against compat verbatim. This is the era's long pole; it re-batches to a later major if it slips more than about two weeks.

**Design notes.** extract state-record type + free functions from class internals (the methods already operate on fields); regenerate `Machine` as mechanical delegation so there is one source of truth; entry-point split + `sideEffects:false`; a bundle-size test asserts the shaken symbols are absent; wc/viz consume the bare functions; manifest entry exists.

Source: era0/WP-3, MP§27, v6_breaking_changes.json#bare-functions-default-api. Part of #1401 (v6 umbrella). cluster `bare-functions`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP6.1.json`.

title: the One Merge (WP6.2)
body:
The v6 integration branch holds finished work that main can't see — vals, tapes, the M3 replayer, codegen — and every release to main widens the gap. One deliberate merge brings it all home as 6.0's feature payload and retires the dual-track branch model permanently, which is the standing toxicity this whole program exists to end. Done when the assembly branch equals main-union-v6 with a green full build and every hand-resolved file documented.

**Design notes.** worktree off main; `git merge origin/v6`; real conflicts expected only in `jssm.ts`/compiler/peg/`package.json` (take the 6.0.0 line); ALL generated artifacts resolve `--theirs` then `npm run build`; verify #773 merged to v6 first; delta-audit note per semantic resolution; escalate on divergent same-behavior edits. This is the payload landing that lets Tjssm-827 (M3) and Tjssm-921 (deno drop) close.

**Dependencies.** blocks Tjssm-827, Tjssm-921.

Source: era0/WP-4. Part of #1401 (v6 umbrella). cluster `one-merge`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP6.2.json`.

title: dragon suite live in CI (WP6.5)
body:
The adversarial grammar-fuzzing tier — the layer above stochastic testing — exists as a harness and one kitchen-sink file, currently dormant. 6.0 wakes it: green in CI, three grammar sections fuzzed at dragon tier, and a doctrine where every confirmed find becomes a permanent minimal regression plus a source fix (never a pinned bug). The full grammar backfill closes by v6's end, and from v7 on every new grammar slice lands pre-fuzzed at birth. This is what hardens the parser before the expression-language grammar lands on it.

**Design notes.** fix bit-rot in `kitchen_sink_dragon.maximal.ts` (print splitmix+fc seeds on failure); lanes per the decided budget (PR fixed-seed 60-90s / nightly 10-15m random / weekly deep with mutation arms); implement the dragons-egg §3/§4/§6 suggestion lists; convention: find -> minimal spec + fix + egg entry; the lane gates on suite completion only, never on generative flake.

**Dependencies.** Depends on Tfsl-65, Tjssm-922.

Source: era0/WP-6, notes/dragons-egg.md. Part of #1401 (v6 umbrella). cluster `dragon`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP6.5.json`.

title: tracker execution — drain, closes, umbrellas (WP6.9)
body:
Both disposition ledgers and the issues projection are approved; this is the mechanical execution of them. It empties the jssm tracker (an npm search-ranking win), files the twelve era umbrellas and six asks on fsl, closes roughly 235 fsl issues each with a living citation to what supersedes it, and milestone-tags the survivors. Done when jssm sits at zero and the fsl tracker mirrors the era program rather than a decade of backlog.

**Design notes.** file projection A/B (milestone-pinned); MIG twins carry a "was jssm#NNN" pointer; every close cites umbrella+artifact (SUP), the satellite twin (SAT), or verified code (DONE?, verify first); filed numbers recorded back into the ledgers.

Source: era0/WP-7, era0/WP-8, issues-projection.md. Part of #1401 (v6 umbrella). cluster `tracker-drain`. era-book item `notes/era-book/items/WP6.9.json`.
